titleAr: أَنَا الَّذِي نَظَرَ الأَعْمَى إِلَى أَدَبِي
titleEn: I Am He Whose Literature the Blind Can See

verses.sadr: أَنَا الَّذِي نَظَرَ الأَعْمَى إِلَى أَدَبِي
يَا مَنْ يَعِزُّ عَلَيْنَا أَنْ نُفَارِقَهُمْ
إِنْ كَانَ سَرَّكُمُ مَا قَالَ حَاسِدُنَا

verses.ajuz: إِنَّ المَعَارِفَ فِي أَهْلِ النُّهَى ذِمَمُ

translationEn: I am he whose literature even the blind can see, and whose words make the deaf hear. I sleep soundly while all creation stays awake debating my verses and quarreling over them. The horse and the night and the desert know me, and the sword and the lance and the paper and the pen. What is he beside whom kingdoms seem small, until one says his greatness has grown immense? O you whom it grieves us to part from — finding everything after them is nothingness. If what our envier said pleased you, then I feel no pain from a wound that satisfies you. Between us, had you honored it, lies a bond of knowing — for among people of wisdom, such bonds are covenants. How often you seek a flaw in us and it defeats you, and God and nobility despise what you attempt.
context: Al-Mutanabbi's most famous poem, composed after his bitter departure from the court of Sayf al-Dawla in Aleppo. The opening three lines became the most quoted boast in Arabic literature. "The horse and the night and the desert know me" is known by virtually every Arabic speaker.

titleEn: According to the Measure of Resolve

verses.sadr: عَلَى قَدْرِ أَهْلِ العَزْمِ تَأْتِي العَزَائِمُ
وَيَطْلُبُ عِنْدَ النَّاسِ مَا عِنْدَ نَفْسِهِ

translationEn: According to the measure of men of resolve come their great deeds, and according to the measure of the noble come their noble acts. Trifles loom large in the eyes of the small, and great things seem small in the eyes of the great. Sayf al-Dawla charges his army with his own ambition, though vast armies have proven unequal to it. He demands of people what he demands of himself, and that is something even lions would not claim. Does the red fortress al-Hadath know its own color, and which of the two cupbearers are the clouds? The white clouds watered it before his descent upon it, but when he drew near, it was watered by skulls.
context: Composed after Sayf al-Dawla's recapture of the fortress of al-Hadath al-Hamra' from the Byzantines in 954 CE. The opening line became one of the most famous proverbs in Arabic, still quoted in speeches and graduation ceremonies throughout the Arab world.

titleEn: If You Venture for Noble Glory

verses.sadr: وَمَا كُلُّ مَا يَتَمَنَّى المَرْءُ يُدْرِكُهُ
verses.ajuz: تَجْرِي الرِّيَاحُ بِمَا لَا تَشْتَهِي السُّفُنُ
بِأَنَّنِي خَيْرُ مَنْ تَسْعَى بِهِ قَدَمُ

translationEn: If you venture for a sought-after glory, do not be content with anything less than the stars. For the taste of death in a trivial matter is the same as the taste of death in a great one. Not everything a man wishes for does he attain — the winds blow contrary to what the ships desire. All those gathered in our assembly shall know that I am the finest man upon whom a foot has ever walked.
context: A collection of al-Mutanabbi's most proverbial wisdom verses. "The winds blow contrary to what the ships desire" became one of the most quoted Arabic proverbs. These verses distill his philosophy of ambition, fate, and self-confidence.

titleEn: O the Burning of My Heart

verses.sadr: مَا لِي أُكَتِّمُ حُبًّا قَدْ بَرَى جَسَدِي
يَا أَعْدَلَ النَّاسِ إِلَّا فِي مُعَامَلَتِي

verses.ajuz: أَنْ تَحْسَبَ الشَّحْمَ فِيمَنْ شَحْمُهُ وَرَمُ

translationEn: O the burning of my heart for one whose heart is cold, and who sees my body and my state as diseased! Why do I conceal a love that has wasted my body, while entire nations claim love for Sayf al-Dawla? If love for his radiant brow unites us all, then would that we were allotted love in proportion to our devotion. O most just of people — except in your treatment of me! In you lies the dispute, and you are both the adversary and the judge. I seek refuge for your sincere glances, lest you mistake for fat one whose flesh is merely swollen.
context: Al-Mutanabbi's most emotionally charged poem, composed during his falling out with Sayf al-Dawla, ruler of Aleppo and his greatest patron. The poem is simultaneously a love letter and a rebuke, blending panegyric with complaint in a way that was unprecedented in Arabic poetry.

titleAr: لَا يَحْمِلُ الحِقْدَ مَنْ تَعْلُو بِهِ الرُّتَبُ
titleEn: He Who Rises Does Not Bear Grudges

verses.sadr: لَا يَحْمِلُ الحِقْدَ مَنْ تَعْلُو بِهِ الرُّتَبُ
verses.ajuz: وَلَا يَنَالُ العُلَا مَنْ طَبْعُهُ الغَضَبُ

verses.sadr: وَمَنْ يَكُنْ ذَا فَمٍ مُرٍّ مَرِيضَةٍ
ذُو العَقْلِ يَشْقَى فِي النَّعِيمِ بِعَقْلِهِ

translationEn: He who rises to high rank does not carry grudges, and he whose nature is anger never attains greatness. He whose mouth is bitter and ailing will find even sweet water bitter. The intelligent man suffers in comfort because of his mind, while the ignorant man lives comfortably in misery. He who accepts humiliation finds humiliation easy — a wound causes no pain to the dead.
context: A collection of al-Mutanabbi's most celebrated wisdom verses (hikam), each of which became an independent proverb in Arabic. "The intelligent man suffers in comfort" and "a wound causes no pain to the dead" remain among the most frequently quoted Arabic maxims to this day.

titleEn: Do You Deny My Brotherhood?
translationEn: Do you deny my brotherhood, O son of Ishaq, and think that someone else's water comes from my vessel? Shall I speak harshly of you after knowing that you are the best of those beneath the sky? I have not yet exceeded twenty years of age, so how have you grown weary of my company? I have not exhausted your description in my praise, so how could I diminish anything of it with satire? Suppose I said: this dawn is night — would the world go blind to the light? You obey the envious ones, though you are a man for whom I would sacrifice myself, while they are my ransom. It is among the wonders that you see me and yet you equate me with less than dust.
context: One of al-Mutanabbi's earliest poems, composed when he was not yet twenty years old. It is addressed to a patron named Ibn Ishaq who had turned against him under the influence of envious rivals, a theme that would recur throughout al-Mutanabbi's life.

titleEn: Do the Watchers Spy on Your Night Visit?

verses.ajuz: قد كان لما كان لي أعضاء

verses.sadr: أنا صخرة الوادي إذا ما زوحمت
من يهتدي في الفعل ما لا تهتدي
لو لم تكن من ذا الورى اللذ منك هو

translationEn: Do the watchers spy on your nocturnal visit? Then let abandoning what they have not taken be a gift. The beautiful one is restless — she is musk that betrays itself, and her movement through the night while she is radiant as the sun. I grieve over my grief, which so bewildered me that knowledge of it is hidden from me. My complaint is the loss of sickness itself, for it existed when I still had limbs to feel it. Your eye carved a wound in my entrails, and they resemble each other — both are wide and beautiful. It pierced through the fine mail upon me, though sometimes the brown spear shatters against it. I am the boulder of the valley when pressed, and when I speak, I am the constellation Gemini. If I am hidden from the fool, it is forgivable — a blind eye cannot see me. It is the way of nights to make my she-camel wonder whether my chest or the desert is wider. Between me and Abu Ali stands the like of him — lofty mountains, and hope as vast as them. And the eagles of Lebanon — how shall I cross it when it is winter, and their summer is itself a winter? The snow has clothed my paths upon it, so that by its whiteness it appears black. Thus the generous man: when he dwells in a land, gold flows and water stands still. He who is guided in deed to what poets cannot find in words until he acts. Every day the rhymes have a turn in his heart, and his ear gives them audience. When you are asked for, it is not because you are in need, and when you conceal your gifts, your blessings betray you. You gave so often that the first giving became unrecognizable — a praise upon God from the grateful. With whichever foot you strode toward glory, the crescent moon is a sandal for your sole. Time is your shield against time, and fate is your ransom against fate. Were you not of this humanity from which you spring, it would be as though its absence were impurities.
context: A panegyric qasida addressed to Abu Ali, likely a patron al-Mutanabbi sought out by crossing the mountains of Lebanon in winter. The poem follows the classical structure: a ghazal opening, a desert journey (rahil), and the praise section. The imagery of crossing Lebanon's snowy passes is vivid and autobiographical.

titleEn: They Have Attributed Affliction to the Tents
translationEn: They have attributed affliction to the tents, but I utterly refuse to accept it. I do not concede the Pleiades above you, nor do I concede the sky above you. You have made the land of Syria desolate by your departure, stripping its abodes of their garment of beauty. Breathe, and though the fortified cities are ten days' journey from you, they recognize the sweetness of that breath in the air.
context: A short panegyric fragment praising a patron whose departure from Syria left the land bereft of its beauty. The reference to al-'Awasim (the fortified frontier cities between the Abbasid caliphate and Byzantium) places this poem in the context of al-Mutanabbi's time at the Syrian-Byzantine border region.

titleEn: Am I a Laughingstock Like the Samaritan?
translationEn: Am I the Samaritan, a laughingstock to every onlooker? Now I see clearly, though I was the most foolish of fools. You were too small for praise, so I said: let me satirize — but no, you were also too small for satire. Before you, I never contemplated the impossible, nor did I test my sword upon dust.
context: A sharp satirical epigram in which al-Mutanabbi dismisses someone as beneath both his praise and his satire. The conceit that the target is too insignificant even for attack is a devastating rhetorical move characteristic of al-Mutanabbi's wit.

titleEn: The Heart Knows Its Own Malady Best

verses.ajuz: دع ما نراك ضعفت عن إخفائه

verses.sadr: ما الخل إلا من أود بقلبه

translationEn: The heart knows its malady better than you, O blamer, and has more right to its eyelid and its tears. By the one I love, I will defy you in passion — I swear by him, by his beauty, and by his splendor. Should I love him and also love being blamed for it? Blame in his regard comes only from his enemies. The slanderers were amazed at the reproachers, who said: leave what we see you too weak to conceal. A true friend is only one I cherish with my heart, seeing through eyes that see through nothing else. He who aids in lovesickness with compassion deserves the mercy of its Lord and his brotherhood more. Be gentle, for rebuke is among love's ailments, and be tender, for hearing is among its limbs. Suppose blame in pleasure is like sleep — it is driven away by his sleeplessness and weeping. The one slain drenched in his tears is like the one slain drenched in his blood. Love, like the beloved, brings sweetness in nearness to the afflicted, yet it reaches into his very soul.
context: A ghazal (love poem) in the classical style, where the poet addresses a reproacher who blames him for his love. The poem is notable for its philosophical treatment of love as an affliction comparable to death, and its defense of passion against the censure of society.

titleEn: The Blame of Blamers Around My Lost Heart

verses.sadr: إن كان قد ملك القلوب فإنه

translationEn: The blamers reproach my wandering heart, while the love of the beloved dwells in its darkest depths. The heart complains of blame's heat to the blaming women, and turns away from revealing its anguish when they reproach. By my very soul, O my blamers, I speak of the king for whose satisfaction I angered all of mankind. If he has conquered hearts, then he is the sovereign of time itself, its earth and its sky. The sun is among his enviers, victory is among his companions, and the sword is among his names. How do those three compare to his three qualities: his beauty, his pride, and his cutting resolve? The ages have passed and never brought his like, and now that he has come, they are powerless to produce his peers.
context: A panegyric that opens with the conventional ghazal prelude of love and blame before pivoting to praise a king. The poem's structure — blaming women, then revealing the beloved is actually a sovereign — is a signature al-Mutanabbi technique that blurs the line between romantic and political devotion.

titleEn: Congratulations Are for Equals

verses.ajuz: بمكان في الأرض أو في السماء

verses.sadr: لا بما يبتني الحواضر في الري
إن في ثوبك الذي المجد فيه

verses.ajuz: لم يكن غير أن أراك رجائي

verses.sadr: فارم بي ما أردت مني فإني

translationEn: Congratulations are only for equals, and for those who bring the distant near. I am part of you — one limb does not congratulate the rest on joyous occasions. These dwellings are too small for you, even if their bricks were stars. You are too exalted in station to be congratulated for any place on earth or in heaven. Yours are the people and the lands and all that moves between the dust and the green sky. The noble Abu al-Misk takes pride only in what he builds of glory, and in his days that passed knowing nothing but battle, and in the marks his white swords left upon the skulls of enemies — not in what builds cities in the countryside or captivates women's hearts. He shames the sun whenever it rises, with a radiant dark sun. In that garment of yours in which glory resides there is a light that eclipses every light. Skin is merely clothing, and whiteness of soul is better than whiteness of robe. Generosity in bravery, intelligence in splendor, power in loyalty. O hope of all eyes in every land — my only hope was to see you. Hurl me at whatever you desire of me, for I am a lion at heart with a human appearance. My heart belongs among kings, even though my tongue is seen among poets.
context: A panegyric addressed to Kafur al-Ikhshidi (Abu al-Misk), the black ruler of Egypt. The poem is remarkable for its explicit defense of Kafur against racial prejudice — 'whiteness of soul is better than whiteness of robe' — while also containing al-Mutanabbi's characteristic self-aggrandizement: 'My heart belongs among kings, even though my tongue is seen among poets.'

titleEn: Let Every Swaggering Walker Be a Ransom
translationEn: Let every slow-swaggering walker be a ransom for every swift-striding one! And every swift delicate Javanese she-camel, swerving aside — it is not their graceful gait I desire, but they are the ropes of life, the undoing of enemies, and the removal of harm. I struck through the wilderness with them like a gambler's throw — for this reason or that. When alarm arose, the horses went first, along with white swords and brown spears. They passed by Nakhl while in our caravan was independence from the world and from it. By evening they offered us the choice between Wadi al-Miyah and Wadi al-Qura. We reached al-Ruhayma in the midst of the night, with more remaining than what had passed. When we dismounted, we planted our spears upon our noble deeds and glory. We spent the night kissing our swords and wiping them clean of the enemies' blood. So that Egypt may know, and those in Iraq, and those in the frontier cities, that I am the true champion. That I was loyal, that I was defiant, that I prevailed over those who opposed me. What laughable things are in Egypt! But it is laughter like weeping. In it is a Nabataean peasant from the lowlands who studies the genealogies of desert folk. And a black man whose lip is half his face, to whom they say: you are the full moon of darkness! And poetry with which I praised the rhinoceros — between verse and incantation. That was no praise for him; rather it was satire of all mankind. A people have gone astray with their idols, but none have gone astray with a wineskin of wind.
context: Composed during al-Mutanabbi's dramatic flight from Egypt after his bitter falling-out with Kafur al-Ikhshidi. This poem is his triumphant declaration of independence as he crosses the desert, simultaneously boasting of his own valor and satirizing Kafur with devastating insults. The 'rhinoceros' and 'black man whose lip is half his face' are direct attacks on Kafur's appearance.

titleAr: ولا عيب فيهم غير أن سيوفهم
titleEn: Their Only Flaw Is That Their Swords Are Notched

verses.sadr: ولا عيب فيهم غير أن سيوفهم

verses.ajuz: إلى اليوم قد جربن كل التجارب

translationEn: There is no flaw in them except that their swords bear notches from striking against battalions. Those swords have been tested since the famed Day of Halima until today, having experienced every trial.
context: One of the most celebrated couplets in Arabic poetry. Al-Mutanabbi praises warriors by saying their only 'flaw' is that their swords are notched from battle — turning a defect into the ultimate compliment. The reference to the Day of Halima connects these warriors to the ancient heroic tradition.

titleEn: I Saw You Bestow Generously upon Poets
translationEn: I saw you bestow generously upon poets, both the modern and the ancient among them. You give those who are alive immense wealth, and you give those who have passed away great honor. I heard you reciting the verses of Ziyad, a recitation as noble as its composer. I did not object to their place in your regard, but I envied his crumbled bones for that honor.
context: A graceful short poem in which al-Mutanabbi praises a patron who honors both living and dead poets. The witty conceit — envying a dead poet's bones for the honor of being recited by the patron — shows al-Mutanabbi's characteristic blend of praise and competitive jealousy.

titleEn: I Wrestle Longing for You, but Longing Prevails

verses.ajuz: وأعجب من ذا الهجر والوصل أعجب

verses.sadr: أما تغلط الأيام في بأن أرى
وكم لظلام الليل عندك من يد
ألا ليت شعري هل أقول قصيدة
وبي ما يذود الشعر عني أقله
verses.ajuz: ولكن قلبي يا ابنة القوم قلب
وإن لم أشأ تملي علي وأكتب

verses.sadr: أبا المسك هل في الكأس فضل أناله
إذا لم تنط بي ضيعة أو ولا ية
يضاحك في ذا العيد كل حبيبه
فإن لم يكن إلا ابو المسك أو هم

translationEn: I wrestle longing for you, but longing prevails, and I marvel at this separation — though reunion would be more marvelous still. Will the days not err in my favor, that I might see a hateful one pushed away or a beloved one drawn near? How many favors has the darkness of night bestowed upon you, proving that the Manichaeans lie about darkness being evil! May you be shielded from enemies' destruction as you march against them by night, while the veiled coquette visits you in its cover. A day long as a lover's night I endured in ambush, watching the sun, waiting for it to set. If only I knew: will I ever compose a poem in which I neither complain nor reproach? The least of what afflicts me would drive poetry from me, yet my heart, O daughter of the people, is still a heart. The virtues of Kafur — when I wish to praise him, and even when I do not wish, they dictate to me and I write. When a man leaves his family behind and heads for Kafur, he does not become a stranger. A youth who fills his deeds with wisdom and judgment, and with rare wit when pleased or angered. His gifts increase the longer one stays, while the waters of the clouds linger and then dry up. O Abu al-Misk, is there any surplus in the cup that I might have? For I have been singing a long while, and you drink. If you do not grant me an estate or a governorship, your generosity clothes me while your distraction strips me bare. On this feast day everyone beside me laughs with their beloved, while I weep and mourn for those I love. I long for my family and yearn to meet them, but where is the phoenix of the west for the yearning man? If there is only Abu al-Misk or them, then you are sweeter and more pleasant in my heart.
context: A major panegyric addressed to Kafur al-Ikhshidi in Egypt during a feast day. Beneath the lavish praise lies al-Mutanabbi's growing frustration: he asks directly for an estate or governorship and admits to homesickness for his family. The poem oscillates between genuine admiration and veiled complaint, foreshadowing his eventual bitter break with Kafur.

titleEn: The Finest Dye for Iron

verses.sadr: أحسن ما يخضب الحديد به أحسن ما يخضب الحديد به

translationEn: The finest dye for iron is blood and wrath that stain it. Do not disgrace it with gold inlay, for water and gold do not mix within it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to disgrace it, to blemish it

context: A pithy epigram about the warrior ethos: a sword should be stained with blood from battle, not decorated with gold. This couplet encapsulates al-Mutanabbi's martial philosophy — that glory comes from action, not ornament.

titleEn: Does He Who Troubles You Know What Ails You?
translationEn: Does he who troubles you know what has disturbed you? And can misfortunes ascend to your celestial sphere? Your body is above the reach of every ailment, so that the approach of the least of them to you is astonishing. How can the world afflict you with anything when you are the physician for the world's own illness? How can complaint visit you with disease when you are the one to whom all turn in calamity? When a disease bewilders Hippocrates himself and no match is found for its victim — through Sayf al-Dawla the Radiant, my eyes rest beneath a sun that never sets. I wage war as he wages war, and by him I have my power; I strike as he strikes, and through him I hit my mark.
context: A poem of concern addressed to Sayf al-Dawla during an illness, blending get-well wishes with panegyric. Al-Mutanabbi argues that disease itself should be ashamed to approach such a great ruler, and compares Sayf al-Dawla to a physician greater than Hippocrates. The final couplet declares the poet's complete identification with his patron.

titleEn: A Black One Whose Heart Is Narrow

verses.sadr: يموت به غيظا على الدهر أهله

translationEn: A black one whose heart is narrow and cowardly, but whose belly is vast. His own people die of rage at fate because of him, as Fatik and Shubayb died of rage. When noble origin, intellect, and generosity are all absent, there is no sweetness to life in your company.
context: A bitter satirical fragment directed at Kafur al-Ikhshidi. The contrast between 'narrow heart' and 'vast belly' is a devastating physical and moral attack. The references to Fatik and Shubayb — warriors who died of frustrated rage — intensify the portrait of Kafur as unworthy of his position.

titleEn: Let My Beloved Ones Fill the Cups
translationEn: Let my beloved ones fill the goblets with pure wine. It is their part to be generous, and mine not to drink — until the cutting verses become the entertainment, and then I shall be transported with joy.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to be ecstatic, transported with joy

context: A short festive piece in which al-Mutanabbi plays on the gathering culture of the Abbasid court. He refuses to drink until his sharp poetry becomes the entertainment, suggesting that his verses intoxicate more than wine.

titleEn: Two Assemblies Distinguished Between Them

verses.sadr: إذا صعدت الى ذا مال ذا رهبا
verses.ajuz: وإن صعدت إلى ذا مال ذا رهبا

translationEn: Two assemblies face each other with a distinction between them, yet one showed better manners. When you ascend to this one, that one inclines in awe, and when you ascend to that one, this one inclines in awe. Why does he fear you when there is no restraint to deter him? I see from their two behaviors something astonishing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the two assemblies, dual sitting places

context: A philosophical observation piece that plays on the symmetry of two assemblies or courts that mirror each other in their deference. The identical second hemistich in the second verse creates a remarkable poetic effect, emphasizing the mutual respect between two powers.

titleEn: The Clouds Presented Themselves as We Returned
translationEn: The clouds presented themselves to me as we returned, so I said: away with you, for I have the rain cloud with me! The hoped-for king in the pavilion caught their scent, and the clouds held back from pouring after they had resolved to rain.
context: A brilliant two-line epigram comparing a generous patron to rain clouds. The conceit is that the king's generosity makes the actual clouds unnecessary — they themselves hold back in deference to his greater bounty. This kind of hyperbolic praise-through-natural-imagery was al-Mutanabbi's signature.

titleEn: Perfume Is Something I No Longer Need
translationEn: Perfume is something I no longer need — the nearness of the prince is fragrance enough. Through him our Lord builds greatness, just as through you He forgives sins.
context: A courtly compliment comparing the prince's presence to perfume — his mere proximity makes artificial fragrance unnecessary. The second verse elevates the patron to an instrument of divine purpose, a common trope in Abbasid panegyric.

titleAr: ألا ما لسيف الدولة اليوم عاتبا
titleEn: Why Is Sayf al-Dawla Displeased Today?

verses.sadr: ألا ما لسيف الدولة اليوم عاتبا
ومالي إذا ما اشتقت أبصرت دونه
وقد كان يدني مجلسي من سمائه
أهذا جزاء الصدق إن كنت صادقا
verses.ajuz: أهذا جزاء الكذب إن كنت كاذبا

verses.sadr: وإن كان ذنبي كل ذنب فإنه
verses.ajuz: محا الذنب كل المحو من جاء تائبا

translationEn: What ails Sayf al-Dawla that he is displeased today? May all creation ransom him — the sharpest of swords in their edges! When I long for him, I see before me wastelands and barren plains that I have no desire for. He used to bring my seat close to his heavens, where I conversed with its moon and stars. Have mercy on one who asks, answer one who calls, and what you grant is enough for me as what you give is enough for you. Is this the reward of honesty, if I was honest? Is this the reward of falsehood, if I was false? If my sin is every sin, then repentance erases all sin completely for one who comes repentant.
context: A poem of reconciliation addressed to Sayf al-Dawla during one of their periodic estrangements. Al-Mutanabbi oscillates between pride and supplication, asking whether the reward for his sincerity (or even his lies) should be exile. The line 'Is this the reward of honesty / Is this the reward of falsehood' is a masterful rhetorical trap that makes the patron's displeasure unjustifiable either way.

titleAr: لما نسبت فكنت ابنا لغير أب
titleEn: When You Were Attributed, You Were Son of No Father

verses.sadr: لما نسبت فكنت ابنا لغير أب
verses.ajuz: ثم اختبرت فلم ترجع إلى أدب
مشتقة من ذهاب العقل لا الذهب

verses.sadr: ملقب بك ما لقبت ويك يه
verses.ajuz: يا أيها اللقب الملقى على اللقب

translationEn: When your lineage was traced, you proved the son of no father; then when tested, you showed no refinement either. You were named 'the Golden One' today, a name derived from the departure of reason, not from gold. You are dubbed with what you were dubbed — alas for you! O you who are a nickname thrown upon a nickname.
context: A devastating satirical epigram in which al-Mutanabbi dismantles a man named 'al-Dhahabi' (the Golden) by punning on the root d-h-b, which means both 'gold' and 'to go away.' His name derives not from gold but from the departure of his mind. The final line — 'a nickname thrown upon a nickname' — denies the target any authentic identity.

titleAr: يا أخت خير أخ يا بنت خير أب
titleEn: O Sister of the Best Brother, O Daughter of the Best Father

verses.sadr: يا أخت خير أخ يا بنت خير أب
غدرت يا موت كم أفنيت من عدد
verses.ajuz: بمن أصبت وكم أسكت من لجب
وكم سألت فلم يبخل ولم تخب

verses.sadr: حتى إذا لم يدع لي صدقه أملا
verses.ajuz: شرقت بالدمع حتى كاد يشرق بي
والبرد في الطرق والأقلام في الكتب

verses.sadr: أرى العراق طويل الليل مذ نعيت
verses.ajuz: فكيف ليل فتى الفتيان في حلب

verses.sadr: وإن تكن خلقت أنثى لقد خلقت

verses.ajuz: فإن في الخمر معنى ليس في العنب

verses.sadr: وليت عين التي آب النهار بها
verses.ajuz: فداء عين التي زالت ولم تؤب
إلا بكيت ولا ود بلا سبب

translationEn: O sister of the best brother, O daughter of the best father — an allusion through them both to the noblest lineage. You have betrayed us, O Death! How many have you destroyed by striking her, and how many clamoring voices have you silenced! How often I accompanied her brother in battle, and how often I asked and he did not withhold, nor was I disappointed. The news traversed the entire peninsula until it reached me — I fled with my hopes to disbelief. But when its truth left me no room for hope, I choked on tears until tears nearly choked on me. Tongues stumbled over the news in mouths, and dispatch riders on roads, and pens in letters. I see Iraq's night has grown long since her death was announced — so how must the night be for the champion of champions in Aleppo? He thinks my heart is not ablaze and my tears are not pouring forth. Indeed they are, by the sanctity of one who honored the sanctity of glory, of those who seek, and of literature. One whose character passed on without being inherited, though her hand's wealth was bequeathed. Her aspiration was in loftiness and glory while growing up, while her peers' concerns were in play and amusement. If she was created female, she was created noble — not female in mind or in lineage. If her stock is Taghlib the mighty, then there is in wine a meaning not found in the grape. Would that the rising of the two suns had set, and the setting of the two suns had not set! Would that the eye of the one whom daylight brought back were ransom for the eye of her who departed and did not return. None who resembled her wore rubies, nor did branches wear Indian swords. Whenever I recalled any kindness from her deeds, I wept — and there is no affection without cause. Whoever contemplates the world and his own soul, thought plants him between helplessness and exhaustion.
context: One of al-Mutanabbi's greatest elegies, composed on the death of the sister of Sayf al-Dawla. The famous line 'I choked on tears until tears nearly choked on me' is among the most quoted in Arabic elegy. The verse 'in wine there is a meaning not found in the grape' became a proverb, arguing that a woman can surpass the nobility of her own tribe, just as wine transcends the grape from which it was made.

titleEn: The Rat Who Sought Refuge Is Now Captured

verses.sadr: لقد أصبح الجرذ المستغير لقد أصبح الجرذ المستغير

translationEn: The rat who sought refuge has now become a captive of fate, struck down by destruction. The Kinani and the Amiri both struck him, and they confronted him in the manner of the Arabs. Both men claimed his killing — so which of you has stolen the noble spoils? And which of you was behind him? For on him there is a bite mark on the tail.
context: A satirical epigram mocking the killing of an enemy whom al-Mutanabbi compares to a rat. The dark humor lies in two warriors competing for credit over killing someone so contemptible. The 'bite mark on the tail' is a vivid final insult.

titleEn: I Reproach You So You May Make Amends
translationEn: I reproach you so that you may make amends, wondering at your own wonderment. For when you met me, you were pained by your own absence from me. And you were so distracted that you could not return my greeting — my preoccupation from you was with you.
context: A delicate three-line ghazal marked by intricate wordplay and the paradox of being so preoccupied with the beloved that one cannot even greet them. The final phrase 'my preoccupation from you was with you' is a celebrated example of Arabic poetic wit.

titleEn: The People Did Not Treat Dabba Fairly
translationEn: The people did not treat Dabba fairly, nor his mother the hag. What I said, I said out of pity, not affection, and as a stratagem for you — you would be excused if you paid attention. What harm is killing to you? It is merely one blow. O killer of every guest whose wealth is buttermilk and a wooden bowl! And the terror of every companion — where behind him is the wonder? Thus were you created, and who is that one, and why would anyone desire him? You were nothing but a fly we swatted away with a flyswatter. If he betrays you — by my life, it is nothing but an insult. If disgraces have become familiar to you, then they are your very lineage. If you understand my meaning, an anguish will lift from you; and if you are ignorant of my meaning, then that ignorance most resembles you.
context: A vicious satirical poem targeting a man named Dabba and his mother. The poem is written in the short, choppy mujtathth meter, which gives it a mocking, sing-song quality perfect for satire. The final couplet is a devastating rhetorical trap: understanding the insult brings relief, but failing to understand it only proves the target's stupidity.

titleEn: May the Branded Steeds Be Your Ransom
translationEn: May the branded steeds be your ransom, and the unsheathed Indian blades! I have described you in travelling verses, yet you remain beyond description however many your attributes. The deeds of all people before you are dark and featureless, while your deed among their deeds is a blaze of white markings.
context: A compact three-verse panegyric using equestrian imagery. The final conceit — that all other men's deeds are like dark, unmarked horses while the patron's deeds are blazing white markings — is drawn from the Bedouin horse-breeding tradition central to Arabic poetic imagery.

titleEn: I See a Keen Blade That Dazzles the Polishers
translationEn: I see a keen blade that dazzles the two polishers, one that every rebellious youth fears. Will you permit me — by your swift-running steeds — to test it for you on this young man?
context: A brief, menacing two-line piece in which al-Mutanabbi admires a sword and asks permission to test it on an enemy. The poem's brevity and directness make it feel like a spontaneous court utterance — a flash of the poet's warrior temperament.

titleEn: Aid with Your Generosity Words I Left Behind

verses.ajuz: في الشرق والغرب من عاداك مكبوتا
وذا الوداع فكن أهلا لما شيتا

translationEn: Support with your generosity words I left behind, which have silenced your enemies in the East and West. I have gazed upon you until the time of my departure has come — this is farewell, so be worthy of what you have willed.
context: A farewell couplet composed upon al-Mutanabbi's departure from a patron's court. The poet asks that his verses — which have already defeated the patron's enemies in East and West — be supported with the generosity they deserve. It captures al-Mutanabbi's characteristic stance: his poetry is a weapon he lends to rulers.

titleEn: A Flock Whose Beauties I Was Denied

verses.sadr: لا سرت من إبل لو اني فوقها
وحملت ما حملت من هذي المها
إني على شغفي بما في خمرها
تكبو وراءك يا ابن أحمد قرح
لا خلق أسمح منك إلا عارف
verses.ajuz: بك راء نفسك لم يقل لك هاتها

verses.sadr: فاليوم صرت إلى الذي لو أنه

translationEn: A flock whose beauties I was denied in person — their attributes near, their possessors far. When they appeared on the ridge, and I cast my eye upon them, I saw faces more delicate than their own tears. My groaning drives their white camels onward behind them — their sighs are mistaken for the urging cries of their camel drivers. They seemed like trees that appeared, but trees from whose fruits I gleaned death. May those camels not travel! Were I upon them, the heat of my tears would have branded away their marks. The gazelle-eyed ones bore what they bore, and I bore what I bore of their sorrows. Though I am passionate about what is in their wine, I remain chaste regarding what is beneath their garments. Manliness, chivalry, and fatherly dignity see every beautiful woman as their rival. Behind you, O son of Ahmad, war-weary steeds stumble — their legs are not among their proper instruments. No character is more generous than yours, except one who knows you, sees your true self, and never says 'give it.' Nobility shows itself standing in your speech, just as the pedigree of horses shows in their voices. You face the blazed foreheads of steeds as if they were lions approaching you from their thickets, and jinn from their hidden places, and wild beasts from their wastelands, and birds from their nests. Mankind was recounted to us and it was a poem, and you were the unique, singular line among its verses. Those whose horsemanship is as fixed as their skin — whose death is like their life, and whose life is like their death. Today I have come to the one who, were he like hearts without their dark cores — one who counts cheaply a glance at him for all that the eye has seen, and a stumble of his foot as worth its blood-price in full.
context: A full-length qasida beginning with a nasib (erotic prelude) describing departing women, then transitioning to praise of a patron called 'son of Ahmad.' The poem showcases al-Mutanabbi's mastery of the classical qasida structure. The line 'Mankind was a poem, and you were its singular verse' is one of the most celebrated compliments in Arabic panegyric.

titleEn: This Day After Tomorrow Bears a Fragrance
translationEn: This coming day carries a fragrance, and a fire rages against the enemy. Under its protection, mothers sleep safely and pilgrims travel their roads unharmed. May your foes remain wherever they are as prey, O stirring lion. I knew you when the ranks were arrayed in battle, and you would not turn aside without your sword. The face of the sea is recognized from afar when it is calm — how much more so when it surges! In a land where the racing courses destroy themselves when the gaps are filled with galloping, you seek the very life of the Roman king, and his foreign subjects sacrifice themselves to ransom him. Do the Christians threaten us with catastrophes, when we are their stars and they are but the towers? Among us is a sword whose bearer is truthful when he meets the foe, and whose raid is relentless. If he advances, we have already visited Samandou, and if he holds back, our appointment is the strait.
context: A panegyric composed for Sayf al-Dawla celebrating a military campaign against the Byzantines. The poem references the fortress of Samandou and the threat to the Roman emperor, reflecting the ongoing Arab-Byzantine frontier wars of the 10th century.

titleEn: A Maiden Whose Body Has No Soul
translationEn: A maiden whose body seems to hold no soul — yet in the heart, her love stirs restless torments. In her hand she holds a bouquet and gestures with it; every fragrance carries the scent of her sweetness. I shall drink the cup at her bidding, while the tears of my eyes pour down my cheeks.
context: A short ghazal fragment in the munsarih meter, depicting a beautiful woman whose ethereal beauty seems soulless yet causes the poet unbearable heartache. The poem blends the imagery of wine-drinking gatherings with love's torment.

titleEn: With Your Faintest Smile, Minds Come Alive

verses.sadr: ومن ذا الذي يقضي حقوقك كلها
verses.ajuz: ومن ذا الذي يرضي سوى من تسامح
فما بال عذري واقفا وهو واضح

verses.sadr: وان محالا اذ بك العيش أن أرى
وما كان ترك الشعر إلا لأنه

translationEn: With your faintest smile, minds come alive, and the limbs of a weak body regain their strength. Who can fulfill all your rights upon him, and whom can you be pleased with except those you graciously forgive? You accept hidden excuses out of noble generosity — so why does my excuse stand rejected when it is plain? It is impossible, while life depends on you, that I should see your body ailing while mine is well. My abandoning poetry was only because praises fall short of describing the prince.
context: A poem of apology and praise addressed to a patron, likely composed when al-Mutanabbi had been absent from composing. The poet explains his silence was not neglect but rather the inadequacy of poetry to capture the prince's greatness.

titleEn: Night Battles Me Fiercely Over You
translationEn: Night battles me fiercely over you, and my departure from you is its sharpest weapon. For whenever I part from my gaze upon you, the distance between my eyelids and the morning grows vast.
context: A compact two-verse ghazal in the wafir meter. Al-Mutanabbi personifies the night as an adversary that weaponizes absence. The sleepless night stretches endlessly when the beloved is out of sight.

titleEn: O Stirrer of Every Soaring Noble Deed
translationEn: O raiser of every soaring noble deed, rider of every swift and graceful mare, thrusting with every broad-bladed spear that plunges deep, defying every sincere advisor who blames you — may God give me to drink, one day before death, the blood of enemies from the depths of their wounds.
context: A short, fierce poem of self-praise (fakhr) in which al-Mutanabbi invokes his warrior identity. The final verse, with its visceral prayer to drink enemy blood from wounds, exemplifies the pre-Islamic martial spirit he frequently channeled.

titleEn: The Rhymes Did Not Slander You
translationEn: The rhymes did not slander you — rather, they obliterated you until you became something that does not exist. It is as though your ear were your mouth when you heard them, and as though, from how intoxicated they made you, they were a sleeping potion.
context: A satirical epigram in which al-Mutanabbi mocks a rival, claiming his poetry did not merely expose the target but erased him from existence. The image of the ear becoming a mouth suggests the victim devoured the verses greedily.

titleEn: Today Is Your Promise — Where Is the Appointed Time?

verses.ajuz: لم تدر أن دمي الذي تتقلد

verses.sadr: قالت وقد رأت اصفراري من به
فرأيت قرن الشمس في قمر الدجى
فله بنو عبد العزيز بن الرضا
من في الأنام من الكرام ولا تقل
verses.ajuz: من فيك شأم سوى شجاع يقصد
لو كان مثلك في سواها يوجد

verses.sadr: كن حيث شئت تسر إليك ركابنا

translationEn: Today was your promised day — so where is the appointed hour? Alas, the day of your promise has no tomorrow. Death is nearer in its claws than your parting, and life is farther from you — do not go far! She who shed my blood with her eyelids did not know that my blood is the necklace she wears. She spoke when she saw my pallor — 'What ails him?' — and she sighed, and I, the sighing one, answered her. She departed, and modesty dyed her whiteness my color, as gold overlays silver. I saw the sun's horn in the moon of darkness, a supple branch bending with another branch upon it. Then war-steeds and neighing chargers, blades and quivering lances, threats and menaces. The nights wore out her affection after us, and time marched over it in fetters. O sickness of the eyelids, you have tormented one so ill that the physician himself fell sick and the healer lost hope. To him belong the sons of Abd al-Aziz ibn al-Rida, and for every caravan there are their camels and the desert waste. Who among the noble can be found in all mankind? Do not say who in you, O Damascus, except a valiant one who is sought. Descriptions grew bewildered at him, for they found his ways growing ever beyond them. In his dignity, his tongue, his fingertips, and his mind — a wonder for all who look closely. A lion whose war-paint is the blood of the fierce lion, a death before whose terror death itself trembles. The night when you arrived there was white — dried blood upon your drawn sword. A land whose honor would equal any other, if your like could be found in any other. Your enemies showed joy at your arrival as though they rejoiced, while doom sat in their midst. You severed them with such envy that I showed them what afflicts them, and they were torn apart envying one who feels no envy. Their multitudes remained as though you were all of them, and you remained among them as though you were alone. Be wherever you wish — our mounts will hasten to you, for the earth is one and you are the one and only. Preserve the sword and do not humble it, for it complains of your right hand, and the skulls bear witness. How can Adam be the father of all creation, when your father and both worlds' lord is Muhammad?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the two weighty things (mankind and jinn)

context: A major qasida of 22 verses in the kamil meter, praising the sons of Abd al-Aziz ibn al-Rida. It opens with a ghazal prelude featuring the beloved and the pain of separation, then transitions into a martial panegyric. The closing hyperbole comparing the patron to Adam and Muhammad reflects al-Mutanabbi's signature grandiose style.

titleEn: I Parted from You

verses.ajuz: قبل الفراق أذى بعد الفراق يد
أعان قلبي على الشوق الذي أجد

translationEn: I parted from you, and if what was harm in your eyes before the parting now becomes a helping hand after it — when I remember what passed between us, it aids my heart against the longing I find within me.
context: A brief, tender fragment of farewell poetry. The paradox at its heart — that what was once harm becomes comfort after separation — captures the way memory transforms pain into something precious when the beloved is gone.

titleAr: عيد بأية حال عدت يا عيد
titleEn: O Eid, in What State Have You Returned?

verses.sadr: عيد بأية حال عدت يا عيد
verses.ajuz: بما مضى أم لأمر فيك تجديد

verses.sadr: لم يترك الدهر من قلبي ولا كبدي

verses.ajuz: أني بما أنا باك منه محسود
من اللسان ، فلا كانوا ولا الجود

verses.sadr: ما يقبض الموت نفسا من نفوسهم
verses.ajuz: إلا وفي يده من نتنها عود
لو أنه في ثياب الحر مولود

verses.sadr: لا تشتري العبد إلا والعصا معه
ما كنت أحسبني أحيا إلى زمن
verses.ajuz: يسيء بي فيه عبد وهو محمود

verses.sadr: ولا توهمت أن الناس قد فقدوا
أم أذنه في يد النخاس دامية

verses.ajuz: في كل لؤم وبعض العذر تفنيد

translationEn: O Eid, in what state have you returned? With what has passed, or is there something new in you? As for loved ones, the desert lies between us — would that between you and me were desert upon desert. Time has left nothing of my heart or liver for any eye or neck to enslave. O my two cupbearers, is it wine in your cups, or is it worry and sleeplessness? Am I a rock? Why does this wine not move me, nor these melodies? What have I endured from this world — and the strangest part is that I am envied for the very thing I weep over! I have alighted among liars whose guest is denied hospitality and forbidden to depart. The generosity of real men comes from their hands, but these men's generosity comes from their tongues — so may neither they nor their generosity endure. Death does not seize a soul among them without holding a stick against the stench in its hand. The eunuch has become the leader of the runaways here — the free man is enslaved and the slave is worshipped. A slave is no fit brother for a free and righteous man, even if he were born in a free man's clothes. Do not buy a slave without a stick in hand, for slaves are filthy and ill-starred. I never thought I would live to a time when a slave could wrong me and be praised for it, nor did I imagine that real men had vanished and that the like of Abu al-Bayda' could exist — that this black man with a pierced lip could be obeyed by these cowardly riffraff. He goes hungry eating my provisions, and detains me so that people will say a great man is sought after. Who taught the black eunuch nobility? Was it his white kinfolk or his noble ancestors? Or was it his ear bleeding in the slave-dealer's hand, or his worth when he was rejected for two pennies? The vilest of the base deserve the excuse of a little wretch — in every baseness, and some excuses are themselves refutation. That is because even the white stallions are incapable of noble deeds, so what of the black castrated ones?
context: Al-Mutanabbi's most infamous poem, a devastating satire against Kafur al-Ikhshidi, the black eunuch ruler of Egypt. Composed during the Eid festival after al-Mutanabbi's bitter disillusionment with Kafur, who had promised him a governorship but never delivered. The poem's extreme racial invective reflects the poet's personal rage and 10th-century attitudes, making it one of the most controversial works in Arabic literary history.

titleEn: O You Who Saw the Wise Man Betrayed
translationEn: O you who saw the wise man betrayed, and saw the noblest of kings made a slave — the wine has weighed heavily upon me, while you are the most guided toward noble deeds. If you graciously grant me leave to depart, I shall count it as a gift from you.
context: A short occasional poem in the mukhlaa al-basit meter, composed at a drinking gathering. Al-Mutanabbi politely asks permission to leave, turning his request into a compliment by calling the host's permission itself a generous gift.

titleAr: ما الشوق مقتنعا مني بذا الكمد
titleEn: Longing Is Not Content with This Anguish of Mine

verses.sadr: ما الشوق مقتنعا مني بذا الكمد
verses.ajuz: حتى أكون بلا قلب ولا كبد

verses.sadr: ولا الديار التي كان الحبيب بها
verses.ajuz: تشكو إلي ولا أشكو الى أحد

verses.sadr: ما زال كل هزيم الودق ينحلها

verses.ajuz: كأن ما سال من جفني من جلدي

verses.sadr: ما دار في خلد الأيام لي فرح
verses.ajuz: أبا عبادة حتى درت في خلدي

verses.sadr: أي الأكف تباري الغيث ما اتفقا
verses.ajuz: حتى إذا افترقا عادت ولم يعد

verses.sadr: لم أجر غاية فكري منك في صفة

translationEn: Longing is not content with this anguish of mine until I am left without heart or liver. The abodes where the beloved once lived complain to me, yet I complain to no one. Every thundering rain cloud kept wearing them down, and sickness kept wasting me, until they came to resemble my body. Whenever my tears overflow, my patience ebbs, as though what flows from my eyelids flows from my very skin. No joy ever crossed the mind of the days for me, O Abu Ubada, until you crossed my mind. A king who, when his treasury fills with wealth, gives it the taste of a mother's bereavement for her child — he empties it entirely. Which hand can rival the rain when they agree? Yet when they part ways, the hand returns while the rain does not. I never stretched the limit of my thought about you in any description without finding its range to be the limit of eternity.
context: A qasida praising Abu Ubada, beginning with a nasib (amatory prelude) about longing and ruined abodes. The poet's comparison of the patron's generosity to rain — with the twist that the hand keeps giving while the rain stops — is a signature al-Mutanabbi conceit.

titleEn: What Farewell Is This — The Farewell of a Grieving Lover

verses.ajuz: فلا عدا الرملة البيضاء من بلد
إن أنت فارقتنا يوما فلا تعد

translationEn: What farewell is this? The farewell of a grieving lover — this is the farewell of the soul from the body. When the wind escorts the clouds upward, may they not pass beyond the white sands of that land. And O parting from the prince of spacious dwelling — if you once part from us, never return!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the loving one, the devoted lover

context: A short farewell poem of great emotional intensity. The opening simile — comparing parting from the beloved to the soul departing the body — became a widely quoted expression in Arabic love poetry.

titleEn: How Many Slain Like Me, a Martyr

verses.sadr: عش عزيزا أو مت وأنت كريم

translationEn: How many slain like me, a martyr to the whiteness of necks and the rose of cheeks! And the eyes of wild cows — though no eyes are like those that slew the baptized lover. By God, have you ever seen full moons that rose behind veils and necklaces, shooting arrows whose feathers are eyelashes, splitting hearts before they pierce skin? They sip from my mouth sips that are sweeter upon it than the profession of God's oneness. Every blood is forbidden to drink, save the son of the grape-cluster. So pour it for me — my soul is ransom for your eyes, O gazelle, and all I own, old and new. My gray hair, my humiliation, my emaciation, and my tears are my witnesses to your love. Every day you gladdened me with union, you frightened me with three days of rejection. My staying in the land of Nakhla is nothing but sleeplessness between my eyelids. So lessen the torment or add to it — this is a life whose bitterness comes swiftly. Forever I traverse the lands while my star is in misfortune and my ambition is in ascendancy. Perhaps I may, by the grace of the Almighty and Praiseworthy, attain some of what I hope for. Live in honor or die nobly, between the thrust of lances and the fluttering of banners. I am a stranger in a nation that God must deliver — like Salih among Thamud.
context: One of al-Mutanabbi's greatest qasidas, composed during his time in Nakhla near Kufa. It opens with a lavish ghazal section before transitioning to his existential complaints about fate and his famous rallying cry: 'Live in honor or die nobly, between the thrust of lances and the fluttering of banners' — one of the most quoted verses in all Arabic poetry. The closing line comparing himself to the Prophet Salih among the doomed Thamud reflects his deep sense of alienation.

titleAr: وشادن روح من يهواه في يده
titleEn: A Fawn Whose Lover's Soul Is in His Hand

verses.sadr: وشادن روح من يهواه في يده
ما اهتز منه على عضو ليبتره

verses.ajuz: ما ذم من بدره في حمد أحمده

verses.sadr: شمس إذا الشمس لاقته على فرس
إن يقبح الحسن إلا عند طلعته
قالت عن الرفد طب نفسا فقلت لها
verses.ajuz: لا يصدر الحر إلا بعد مورده

verses.sadr: لم أعرف الخير إلا مذ عرفت فتى
verses.ajuz: لم يولد الجود إلا عند مولده

verses.sadr: نفس تصغر نفس الدهر من كبر
verses.ajuz: لها نهى كهله في سن أمرده

translationEn: A fawn in whose hand lies the soul of his lover, wearing the sword of rejection at the height of his neck — whenever it quivered against a limb to sever it, the lover shielded himself with the armor of endurance. Time brought him, among those he loved, something it found faulty in its full moon, within the praise of its most praiseworthy. A sun — when the sun met him on horseback, its light wavered from his own radiance. Beauty itself turns ugly except before his countenance, as the slave is ugly except before his master. She said, 'Be content without the gift,' and I replied, 'A free man does not leave until he has drunk his fill.' I never knew goodness until I knew a youth at whose birth generosity itself was born. A soul whose greatness makes the soul of time seem small, possessing the wisdom of a mature man while still in the age of beardless youth.
context: A qasida opening with a ghazal image of the beloved as a fawn wielding the sword of rejection, then praising a young patron whose generosity and wisdom surpass his age. The verse about the free man refusing to leave without receiving his due reflects al-Mutanabbi's proud self-image.

titleEn: Is This Musawir or the Sun's Horn?

verses.sadr: شم ما انتضيت فقد تركت ذبابه
لم يلق قبلك من اذ اختلفت القنا

translationEn: Is this Musawir, or the horn of the sun, or a lion of the thicket advancing ahead of the master? Smell what you have drawn — you have left its blade in pieces and left the people shattered. Suppose you crushed Ibn Yazdad and his companions — do you think all mankind have become sons of Yazdad? You left their faces where you met them turned into their backs, and their livers into fragments. In a stand where death halted over them in tight straits and seized them utterly — their souls froze, and when you came upon them, you set them flowing and gave them steel to drink. Between Karkhaya and Kalwadha, in armor, with your father's brother Muadh — you silenced their tongues by striking their necks so swiftly that your resolve matched its execution. No one before you, when the lances clashed, made the thrust of spears a refuge from spears.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its blade tip, edge of the sword

context: A battle panegyric praising a warrior named Musawir for his devastating victory over Ibn Yazdad. The poem references specific Iraqi locations — Karkhaya and Kalwadha near Baghdad — and celebrates the warrior's ferocity with characteristically violent imagery. The closing conceit of making spear-thrusts a refuge from other spear-thrusts is quintessentially Mutanabbi.

titleEn: By Hope in Your Generosity, Poverty Is Driven Away

verses.ajuz: إلا الإل ه وأنت يا بدر

translationEn: By hope in your generosity, poverty is driven away, and by your hostility, lifetimes are cut short. The glass goblet boasted that you drank from it, and the wine itself looked down upon those who refused her. You remained sober while she intoxicated us, as though drunkenness itself feared you. No one is hoped for in noble deeds except God and you, O Badr.
context: A short panegyric addressed to a patron named Badr, composed at a wine gathering. The conceit that even drunkenness is afraid of the patron elevates a drinking-scene compliment into hyperbolic praise, a hallmark of al-Mutanabbi's wit.

titleEn: Your Pleasure Is the Pleasure I Prefer
translationEn: Your pleasure is the pleasure I prefer, and your secret is my secret — I reveal nothing. Your manliness suffices you against what you fear, and your friendship secures you against what you dread. Your secret in my breast is dead — even if secrets are resurrected, this one shall not rise. It is as though my eyes rebelled against you and I concealed from the heart what they see. To reveal what I am entrusted with would be treachery, and a free man does not betray. When I am capable of uttering something, I am even more capable of refraining. I command my soul as I wish and master it even when the lances are reddened with blood. Turn by turn, O sword of its destiny, and yours is the command, O best of those who command. Your messenger came to me in haste, and my stored-up poetry answered his call. Had it been a dark day of battle, my sword and my chestnut steed would have answered instead. May fate never neglect its own people, for you are the eye through which it sees.
context: A poem of loyalty and trust addressed to a patron, in which al-Mutanabbi pledges absolute discretion about the patron's secrets. The poem interweaves themes of honor, trustworthiness, and martial readiness, reflecting the dual role of the court poet as both literary servant and potential warrior.

titleEn: The Prince — May God Prolong His Reign

verses.sadr: في الشرب جارية من تحتها خشب
verses.ajuz: ما كان والدها جن ولا بشر

verses.sadr: قامت على فرد رجل من مهابته
verses.ajuz: وليس تعقل ما تأتي وما تذر

translationEn: The prince — may God prolong his reign — is a glory that has clothed the tribe of Mudar in pride. At the drinking session there is a maiden standing upon wood, whose father was neither jinn nor human. She stands on one leg out of awe of him, and she does not comprehend what she does or leaves undone.
context: A playful riddle-poem describing a wine jug or bottle at a gathering — the 'maiden standing on wood whose father was neither jinn nor human' and who 'stands on one leg' in awe. Such riddle descriptions (wasf) were a popular poetic game at Abbasid courts.

titleEn: Fasting and Feasting, Festivals and Ages

verses.ajuz: فما يخص به من دونها البشر

verses.sadr: ما الدهر عندك إلا روضة أنف
verses.ajuz: يا من شمائله في دهره زهر

verses.sadr: ما ينتهي لك في أيامه كرم

translationEn: Fasting and feasting, festivals and ages are all illuminated by you — even the sun and the moon. The crescents see a face whose bounty is universal; what he bestows is not reserved for humans alone. Time in your presence is nothing but a fresh garden, O you whose character blooms like flowers in your era. Your generosity knows no end in its days, so may your years and life know no end. Your share of time's recurrence is honor, while others' share of it is gray hair and old age.
context: A festival panegyric (Eid poem) praising a patron's generosity, likely composed for Eid al-Fitr. Al-Mutanabbi contrasts the patron's eternal gain of honor from passing time with ordinary people who gain only aging and gray hair — a bittersweet meditation on mortality wrapped in praise.

titleAr: ظلم لذا اليوم وصف قبل رؤيته
titleEn: To Describe This Day Before Seeing It Is Injustice

verses.sadr: ظلم لذا اليوم وصف قبل رؤيته
verses.ajuz: لا يصدق الوصف حتى يصدق النظر

verses.sadr: تزاحم الجيش حتى لم يجد سببا
verses.ajuz: إلى بساطك لي سمع ولا بصر

translationEn: To describe this day before witnessing it is injustice — description cannot be true until the eye confirms it. The army crowded so densely that neither my hearing nor my sight could find a path to your carpet. I was present in special audience yet absent from it, an eyewitness whose witnessing was all hearsay. Today the Roman emperor lifts his gaze, for your pardon of him counts as his victory. If you reply to his letters with anything, he will forever boast of it before kings. For a time, their necks have rested from swords, and the rest of the people await their turn. Others have replaced them so that the heads and the palace may rest. To liken your generosity to morning rains is itself a second act of generosity that your hand bestows upon the rain. The sun gains its light from you as it rises, just as the moon gains its light from the sun.
context: A panegyric for Sayf al-Dawla celebrating a diplomatic triumph over the Byzantine emperor. The poem captures the grandeur of a royal audience so packed that the poet himself could barely see. The final image — the sun gaining its light from the patron — inverts the cosmic order as the ultimate hyperbole.

titleEn: I Know Full Well — and the Wise Man Is Knowing

verses.sadr: ما كنت أحسب قبل دفنك في الثرى
ما كنت آمل قبل نعشك أن أرى

translationEn: I know full well, and the wise man is knowing, that life, however much you cling to it, is an illusion. I have seen everyone console himself with some pretext, though all march toward annihilation. O neighbor of the dark dungeon, held in a pit where the only light and radiance are from his face — I never thought before your burial in the earth that stars could sink into the dust. I never hoped before your bier to see Mount Radwa carried upon the hands of men. They bore him out, and behind every mourner were the thunderbolts of Moses on the day the Mount was leveled. The sun hung sick in the belly of the sky, and the earth trembled as though about to heave. The rustling of angel wings surrounded him, and the eyes of the people of Latakia were frozen wide. Until they reached a grave whose tomb seemed carved in the heart of every believer. With provisions — decay has shrouded from his kingdom only a helmet, and the kohl of his eyes is camphor. In him were generosity, eloquence, piety, valor entire, wisdom, and goodness. It is as though Jesus son of Mary were his story, and as though Lazarus were his buried form.
context: A powerful elegy (ritha) for a great figure buried in Latakia, opening with philosophical reflections on the illusion of life before depicting the cosmic funeral procession. The comparison of the dead man to the stars sinking into dust and his bier to Mount Radwa carried by men are among al-Mutanabbi's most striking images. The closing reference to Jesus and Lazarus implies hope for the deceased's memory to be resurrected.

titleEn: He Got from Me What I Got from Him
translationEn: He got from me what I got from him — God knows what wine does! And as for my departure to my quarters — do I have your permission, O prince?
context: A witty two-verse occasional poem composed at the end of a wine gathering. The mutual intoxication — 'he got from me what I got from him' — and the formal request for permission to leave create a charming snapshot of courtly etiquette.

titleEn: Leaving Your Praise Is Like Satire Against Myself
translationEn: Leaving off your praise is like satire against myself, and even abundant praise is too little for you. Yet I abandoned brief poetry for a reason that one like me is excused for. Your noble traits have so outstripped my words, and generosity has so altered my speech, that may God water those you love through your hands, and may He give you to drink, O prince.
context: A poem of apology and praise in which al-Mutanabbi explains that his silence was not neglect — rather, the patron's qualities so exceeded what words could capture that the poet was rendered speechless. The paradox of praising by apologizing for insufficient praise is a sophisticated rhetorical move.

titleAr: زعمت أنك تنفي الظن عن أدبي
titleEn: You Claimed You Would Dispel Doubt About My Art

verses.sadr: زعمت أنك تنفي الظن عن أدبي

translationEn: You claimed you would dispel doubt about my literary art, and you are the greatest person on earth in stature. I am the gold whose quality is well known — smelting only adds dinar upon dinar to its value.
context: A sharp two-verse epigram of self-praise in which al-Mutanabbi compares himself to gold that only increases in value with testing. The implication is clear: no examination or challenge will diminish him, only prove his worth further.

titleAr: باد هواك صبرت أم لم تصبرا
titleEn: Your Love Is Plain, Whether You Endured or Not

verses.sadr: باد هواك صبرت أم لم تصبرا
verses.ajuz: وبكاك ان لم يجر دمعك أو جرى
لما رآه وفي الحشا ما لا يرى

verses.sadr: لو كنت أفعل ما اشتهيت فعاله
يا من إذا ورد البلاد كتابه
أنا من جميع الناس أطيب منزلا

verses.ajuz: لو كان منك لكان أكرم معشرا

translationEn: Your love is plain, whether you endured or not, and it weeps for you whether your tears flowed or held back. How often your patience and smile deceived a companion who saw you while within your breast lay what cannot be seen. The heart commanded its tongue and eyelids to conceal, and your body alone was informer enough. Time offered its gifts but I did not accept them; it willed one thing for me, and I chose to be selective. Onward to Arrajan, O steeds, for this is my resolve that leaves the lances shattered. Had I done what I desired, your star would not have pierced the murky dust-cloud. I am heading toward Abu al-Fadl, the righteous — I have sworn to seek the greatest ocean for gems. If his cavalry and arms do not aid me, when shall I ever lead an army against my enemies? O you whose letter, when it reaches a land before his armies, turns those armies back in bewilderment — you are the sole rider when you take a path, and who would ride behind one mounted on a lion? Your qualities left in the eye what his speech left — like writing that fills the ears of one who sees it. Time's hand hastened toward you as though it found him preoccupied and deep in thought. Who will tell the Bedouins that after them I sat with Aristotle and Alexander? I am, of all people, the most pleasant in dwelling, the happiest in mount, and the most profitable in trade. Saturn, though the planets are his people — had he been of your stock, he would have been the most honored of company.
context: A major qasida composed on the road to Arrajan (in Persia), praising Abu al-Fadl. The poem is notable for its reference to sitting with Aristotle and Alexander — an allusion to the philosophical and literary culture of the Buyid courts. The closing comparison of the patron to Saturn elevates him above the celestial bodies.

titleEn: I See That Closeness Has Turned to Estrangement

verses.ajuz: ت إن كان ذلك مني اختيارا
ل هم حمى النوم إلا غرارا
ولا أنا أضرمت في القلب نارا
ت لا يختصصن من الأرض دارا

verses.sadr: ولي فيك ما لم يقل قائل
verses.ajuz: وما لم يسر قمر حيث سارا

verses.sadr: ومن كنت بحرا له يا علي

translationEn: I see that our closeness has turned to estrangement, and the long greeting has become brief. You have left me today in such shame that I die over and over and come alive over and over. I steal glances at you bashfully and silently rein in my horse among the cavalry. I know that when I apologize to you, my apology itself requires an apology. I would have denied your dazzling noble deeds if my absence were by choice. But worry has forbidden poetry save a little, as worry has forbidden sleep save a doze. I did not make my body ill, nor did I set fire to my heart. So do not blame me for time's sins — it wronged me and harmed none but me. I have for you verses that wander the world and favor no land over another. Rhymes that, when they depart from my tongue, leap over mountains and wade through seas. I have in you what no speaker has said, and what no moon has ever carried in its course. If people were created from their era, they would be the darkness and you the daylight. And for one whose ocean you are, O Ali, he will accept only the largest pearls.
context: A qasida addressed to Ali, combining apology for poetic silence with self-confident praise. Al-Mutanabbi blames illness and misfortune for his absence, not any lack of devotion. The imagery of verses leaping mountains and wading seas captures the poet's belief in the immortality and reach of his work.

titleEn: The Clear Wine Passed to You, O Son of Ibrahim

verses.ajuz: فشبهتها بالشمس في البدر في البحر

verses.sadr: إذا ما ذكرنا جوده كان حاضرا
verses.ajuz: نأى أودنا يسعى على قدم الخضر

translationEn: The clear wine passed to you, O son of Ibrahim, and I congratulated her on a drinker who intoxicates intoxication itself. I saw the wine in the glass in his hand and likened it to the sun in the full moon upon the sea. Whenever we mention his generosity, it comes running — though far away, it hastens on the feet of al-Khidr.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Khidr (the immortal guide in Islamic tradition)

context: A short wine-scene poem praising Ibn Ibrahim at a gathering. The layered simile — wine as the sun in the full moon upon the sea — creates a triple image of luminous beauty. The reference to al-Khidr's miraculous speed poetically describes how the patron's generosity arrives the moment it is mentioned.

titleEn: Remnants of a People Who Foretold Their Own Ruin

verses.ajuz: قرى كل ضيف بات عند سوار

translationEn: We are the remnants of a people who foretold their own ruin, and emaciated travelers like drinkers of wine. We halted at the mercy of the winds in a mosque where pebbles and dust clothed us as their garments. My two companions, this is no resting place for the likes of us — tighten the saddles and depart by daylight! And do not be surprised at the howling winds, for they are the hospitality offered to every guest who spends the night at Suwar.
context: A poem of travel and hardship composed during one of al-Mutanabbi's journeys, likely on the road between Iraq and Persia. The image of weary travelers sheltering in a mosque while the wind pelts them with sand captures the harsh realities of 10th-century desert travel. The ironic description of the wind as 'hospitality' is characteristic of his dark humor.

titleAr: لا تنكرن رحيلي عنك في عجل
titleEn: Do Not Blame My Hasty Departure

verses.sadr: لا تنكرن رحيلي عنك في عجل

verses.ajuz: يوم الوغى غير قال خشية العار

translationEn: Do not condemn my hasty departure from you, for I did not choose to leave of my own will. Sometimes a man parts with his very soul on the day of battle, not out of indifference but out of fear of shame. I have been afflicted with envious enemies whom I must fight — so let your generosity be one of my allies against them.
context: A short poem of farewell addressed to a patron, in which al-Mutanabbi explains that his departure is compelled by enemies and envy, not by choice. He asks the patron's generosity to serve as an ally in his ongoing struggle.

titleEn: My Excuse from Virgin Affairs

verses.sadr: فقل في حاجة لم أقض منها

translationEn: Seek my excuse from these virgin affairs that have settled in my ribs in place of maidens in their chambers. The smiles of the battles of our age come from swords, not from mouths. I rode toward them with sleeves rolled up, upon every sturdy steed with braided mane. Sometimes my saddle rests in Bedouin tents, and sometimes upon the frame of a camel. I expose my chest to unyielding spears and set my face against the burning noon. I travel alone through the darkness of night as though I walk within a luminous moon. Speak of a need of which I have not fulfilled even a date-stone's worth, despite my passion for it. If I were envied for something precious, I would give it freely to the fortunate seeker. But I am envied for my very life — and what good is life without joy? O son of Kurus, you half-blind one — and if you boast, then you are half-sighted. You oppose us because we are not stammerers, and you hate us because we are not one-eyed. Had you been a man worth satirizing, we would have satirized you — but a span is too narrow for a journey.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: not even a date-stone's worth (nothing at all)

context: A poem combining self-praise and satire. Al-Mutanabbi recounts his hardships of travel and battle, then turns to attack a one-eyed rival (Ibn Kurus), mocking him for envying what he cannot match. The poem showcases al-Mutanabbi's characteristic pride and contempt for lesser men.

titleEn: Easy Now, O Plain Watered by Showers
translationEn: Easy now, O wide plain — may you be watered with showers. You have left the eyes of my servants bewildered. They mistook the ostriches upon you for palm trees, and the herds of wild cattle for lighthouses. My companions gripped their saddles as laughter overtook them and ran wild.
context: A light descriptive poem about a vast desert plain that confuses al-Mutanabbi's traveling companions, who mistake ostriches for palm trees and wild cattle for minarets due to the shimmering mirages. The humor is rare for al-Mutanabbi, showing a playful side.

titleEn: You Command Seclusion Behind a Veil

verses.ajuz: لم يحجبا لم يحتجب عن ناظر

translationEn: You have taken to commanding seclusion for privacy — how impossible! You are not capable of being veiled. He whose radiant brow and generous giving cannot be concealed cannot be hidden from any eye. Even when you withdraw behind a curtain, you are not hidden; even when you are concealed, you are the very essence of what is manifest.
context: A short panegyric addressed to a patron who has adopted a policy of seclusion. Al-Mutanabbi argues with elegant paradox that the patron's radiance and generosity are so great that no veil can hide him — his hidden state is itself a form of manifestation.

titleEn: Do Not Blame the One Who Sees the Sun
translationEn: Do not blame the one who sees the sun and does not deny it. Rather, blame lies on the one who reckons it darkness after he has already seen it clearly.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: deny it, refuse to recognize it
one who reckons it, considers it

context: A pithy wisdom couplet. Al-Mutanabbi argues that blame belongs not to the one who acknowledges an obvious truth, but to the one who, having seen the truth with his own eyes, willfully calls it darkness. A defense of honest recognition over self-deluding denial.

titleEn: Like My Sword's Watered Pattern

verses.sadr: تحسب الماء خط في لهب النا

verses.ajuz: ولو أني له إلى الشمس عاز

verses.sadr: وحكى في اللحوم فعلك في الوف

translationEn: Like my own pattern is the watered steel of my cutting sword — a delight to the eye and a weapon for combat. You would think the water-mark was drawn in flame, the finest of lines within its folds. Whenever you seek its color, waves upon the blade prevent the eye, as if mocking you. Fine as floating motes of dust, elegant, flowing along a level surface that shimmers. The straps of time have carried it until they need a cobbler to mend them. O remover of my darkness, my garden on the day of drinking, my fortress in the duel! O Yemeni blade — if I could, my very eye would be its sheath, out of reverence. My lightning, when I flash, is my deeds; my clanging, when I clash, is my war-chant. I did not carry you marked like this except to strike necks and waists, and to cut through the iron upon them — for each of us invades his own kind today. The galloping drew it after nightfall in Najd, and the people of the Hijaz looked up expecting rain. I wished for its equal, but it was as though I sought a match for Ibn Salih. Not every nobleman is al-Rudhbari, nor is every bird that flies a falcon. My horseman wears a crown of glory that was once a jewel upon Anushirwan. His soul is above every noble lineage, even if I traced it to the sun. The beauty of lofty deeds has occupied his heart away from the beauty of faces and figures. Gems and pearls and rubies are as though they were minted from his speech and drawn from his treasure. His enemies gnaw on embers and iron before reaching him, as though chewing Ahwaz sugar. O you whose courtyard is vast, yet within it there is no lodging for any passing king! Through you the tips of spearheads have become to me like the legs of swarming locusts. Your sword has imitated in flesh your deeds with wealth, and destroyed the sturdy, compact mare. Among people there are those upon whom poets pass whose verse is like the buzzing of flies. They think themselves discerning in this art, yet they are blind men who have lost their canes. Every poem mirrors its composer in describing you, and the mind of the one who approves reveals the mind behind the metaphor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: watered pattern on a sword blade (damascening)

context: A major qasida praising Ibn Salih al-Rudhbari. Al-Mutanabbi begins with an elaborate description of his sword, then pivots to praise his patron as a peerless warrior and nobleman. The closing verses are a literary-critical statement: every poem reflects its composer, and only a discerning mind can judge true poetry.

titleEn: An Ear That Reminded No One
translationEn: O ear, you reminded no one who had forgotten, nor did you soften a heart that was hard. And the prince was not distracted from lofty deeds, nor from the right of his Creator, by any cup of wine.
context: A brief panegyric fragment praising a prince's devotion to noble pursuits and piety, noting that neither wine nor distraction can divert him from greatness or worship.

titleEn: Sweeter Than Aged Wine
translationEn: Sweeter than aged wine and more delicious than the passing of goblets is the exchange of sword-blades and lances, and my hurling one army into another. My death in battle is my life, for I have seen that true living lies in fulfilling the soul's desires. And if I were to share my cup with a companion who delighted me, it would be Abu Dubays.
context: A poem of self-praise in which al-Mutanabbi declares that the clash of weapons is sweeter to him than wine. The famous line 'My death in battle is my life' encapsulates his warrior philosophy that true living means fulfilling the soul's highest ambitions.

titleEn: Standing Upon Heads Is Little for Him
translationEn: Standing upon heads in his honor is too little for him, and offering the dearest of souls is barely enough. If you betrayed him on a day of smiles, how would you fare on a day of frowning?
context: A couplet praising a patron so exalted that even the greatest sacrifices are insufficient tribute. The second verse warns that disloyalty in times of ease will be catastrophic in times of hardship.

titleEn: More Foolish Than a Slave and His Wife

verses.sadr: ما من يرى أنك في وعده

verses.ajuz: ولا يعي ما قال في أمسه

translationEn: More foolish than a slave and his wife is the one who gives a slave authority over himself. His empowerment only reveals how corruption has overtaken his senses. The one who sees you as keeping a promise is not like the one who sees you as a captor. He does not fulfill his pledge today, nor remember what he said yesterday. Do not hope for good from a man over whose head the slave-dealer's hand has passed. If doubt assails you about his character, look to his origins. Rarely does a man prove base in his garments unless he is base in his stock. He who finds a path away from his worth will not find a path away from his origins.
