context: A fierce satirical poem attacking a man of servile origin who has been given power. Al-Mutanabbi argues that a slave's nature cannot be changed by status — corruption reveals itself, and lineage determines character. This reflects the poet's aristocratic worldview.

titleEn: She Appeared and Stirred a Fever

verses.sadr: وجعلت حظي منك حظي في الكرى
يا من نلوذ من الزمان بظله

verses.ajuz: من في العراق يراك في طرسوسا

translationEn: She appeared to us and stirred a hidden fever, then turned away without healing the faintest breath. She made my share of her equal to my share of sleep, and left me sitting with the two guardian stars. She cut through that veil with an intoxication and poured out the cups of separation's wine. If you are departing, then my tears are enough to fill your water-skins and quench your white camels. Far be it from one like you to be miserly, and from a face like yours to be frowning, and from a union like yours to be withheld, and from a gift like yours to be paltry. A soft maiden who ignited war between me and my reproachers, leaving my heart a trampled battlefield. A fair woman whose coquetry prevents her from speaking — and when I saw her army of charm, I saw a legion. When I found the cure for my illness with her, the prescriptions of Galen became worthless to me. Zurayq kept Muhammad for the frontier posts — the precious one preserved the precious for precious ends. When he settles, his wealth leaves the treasuries; when he marches, heads leave their bodies. A king — if you oppose yourself, he opposes you; and if you are content, the most fearsome of what you dread becomes a companion. O you in whose shade we shelter from fate forever, and by whose name we drive away Iblis! Truthful is the one who speaks of you though he falls short of your description — one in Iraq sees you in Tarsus. I have scattered pearls upon you, so examine them — forgers are many, so beware of fraud. The best of birds dwell on palaces, and the worst inhabit ruins and charnel houses. If the world were generous, it would ransom you with its people; and if it struggled, it would inscribe itself as your devoted captive.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hidden fever, faint trace of love

context: A mixed qasida that opens with a ghazal (love prelude) about a woman who stirs longing then departs, before transitioning to panegyric praise of a patron named Muhammad at the frontier. Al-Mutanabbi closes with a literary claim: his poems are genuine pearls amid a sea of counterfeit verse.

titleEn: My Night in Damascus on a Bed of Fire

verses.sadr: سقى الدم كل نصل غير ناب

translationEn: My night in Damascus is on a mattress stuffed, for me, with the burning heat of my own innards. I met a night black as a gazelle's eye, and grief like wine seeping into my marrow. And longing like a blaze within a heart like embers in ribs like a roasting pit. Blood has watered every blade that is not dull, and drenched every spear that is not feeble. He has become known as the Father of Calamities, as though Abu al-Asha'ir were not already famous. And al-Husayn has been surnamed by what he is called: the doom of heroes, the rain-cloud of the thirsty. They met him bare-headed in an armor of blows, finely woven and blazing at its edges. As though upon their skulls his sword was fire, and their hands the wings of moths. The hooves of the horses bloody one another, yet there is no sign of stumbling on their pasterns. And the leader among them stood alone, unafraid, though his army and his reinforcements were far. The arrows bending in his body were like palm-fronds twisted in the branches of their nests. O sea of seas — and I do not exaggerate — O king of kings — and I do not flatter! It is as though you gaze into every heart, so that no cheater's place is hidden from you. Shall I be patient away from you, when you have never been stingy with anything, nor accepted the words of a slanderer against me? Any cavalry you rode among would charge, even if they were Nabataean peasants on donkeys. I see people as darkness and you as light, and among them I am one who lives only for you. I was afflicted by them as a rose is met by noses more deserving of nose-rings. They cling to you when they grow lean with the nights, and when they fatten, they snarl around you. News of the prince arrived and they said 'Return!' — I said 'Yes, even if they had reached the farthest lands.' No longing was ever found like my longing, no eagerness ever known like my eagerness. I journeyed to you in pursuit of glory, while others journeyed in pursuit of livelihood.
context: Addressed to Abu al-Asha'ir al-Husayn, this qasida was composed in Damascus. Al-Mutanabbi describes his sleepless anguish, then praises the patron's battlefield heroics in vivid imagery. The final verse became proverbial: 'I journeyed to you seeking glory, while others journeyed seeking bread.'

titleAr: إذا اعتل سيف الدولة اعتلت الأرض
titleEn: When Sayf al-Dawla Falls Ill, the Earth Falls Ill

verses.sadr: إذا اعتل سيف الدولة اعتلت الأرض

verses.ajuz: فإنك بحر كل بحر له بعض

translationEn: When Sayf al-Dawla falls ill, the earth falls ill, and all upon it, and courage and pure generosity. How can I benefit from sleep when, because of his illness, even the closing of eyes has become sick? May He who heals His creation through your generosity heal you, for you are a sea of which every other sea is but a part.
context: Composed during an illness of Sayf al-Dawla, the Hamdanid prince of Aleppo and al-Mutanabbi's most celebrated patron. The poet's concern is cosmic: when the patron is ill, the whole earth is ill. This reflects the deep bond between the two before their eventual falling out.

titleAr: مضى الليل والفضل الذي لك لا يمضي
titleEn: The Night Has Passed but Your Virtue Endures

verses.sadr: مضى الليل والفضل الذي لك لا يمضي
verses.ajuz: ورؤياك أحلى في العيون من الغمض
شهيد بها بعضي لغيري على بعضي
تخص به يا خير ماش على الأرض

translationEn: The night has passed, but your virtue does not pass away, and the sight of you is sweeter to the eyes than sleep itself. I have been collared by a blessing from you to which part of me testifies against the rest. May the peace of Him whose throne is above the heavens be upon you especially, O best of any who walks upon the earth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I was collared, encircled (by a blessing)

context: A short poem of thanks and praise, likely addressed to Sayf al-Dawla after receiving a gift. The poet acknowledges the patron's favor with a prayer invoking divine peace upon him, calling him the best person walking on earth.

titleEn: You Have Done to Us What the Sky Does to Its Earth

verses.ajuz: في الجود بان مذيقه من محضه

translationEn: The prince's robes of honor have done to us what the sky does to its earth — and his right upon us we have not yet fulfilled. It is as though the soundness of their weave comes from his word, and the beauty of their purity from his own nature. When you entrust a noble man to his own judgment in generosity, the diluted shows itself from the pure.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: robes of honor, gifts of clothing

context: A poem of gratitude for robes of honor received from a prince. Al-Mutanabbi compares the patron's gifts to rain from heaven upon earth, and argues that the quality of the gift reflects the quality of the giver's character.

titleEn: The Last Breath of a Soul Bidding Farewell

verses.sadr: حشاي على جمر ذكي من الهوى
verses.ajuz: وعيناي في روض من الحسن ترتع

verses.sadr: أتت زائرا ما خامر الطيب ثوبها
فما جلست حتى انثنت توسع الخطى
فشرد إعظامي لها ما أتى بها
فيا ليلة ما كان أطول بتها
تذلل لها واخضع على القرب والنوى
verses.ajuz: فما عاشق من لا يذل ويخضع

verses.sadr: ولا ثوب مجد غير ثوب ابن أحمد
بذي كرم ما مر يوم وشمسه
verses.ajuz: على رأس أوفى ذمة منه تطلع

verses.sadr: فتى ألف جزء رأيه في زمانه
فصيح متى ينطق تجد كل لفظة

verses.ajuz: لما فاتها في الشرق والغرب موضع
على أنه من ساحة الأرض أوسع

verses.sadr: وقلبك في الدنيا ولو دخلت بنا
ألا كل سمح غيرك اليوم باطل

translationEn: The last breath of my soul bid farewell the day they departed, and I did not know which of the travelers to follow. My insides burn on glowing coals of passion, while my eyes graze in a garden of beauty. She came visiting — no perfume had touched her garment, yet musk wafted from her sleeves. She had barely sat down before she turned to leave with widening steps, like a Fatima weaned from her pearl before she could nurse. My awe of her drove away the sleep that had brought her, and my stricken heart was consumed. O what a night — how long was its lingering — and the venom of serpents is sweet compared to what I swallow. Be humble before her and submit, whether near or far, for no one is a lover who does not abase himself. No garment of glory rests on anyone but Ibn Ahmad without being patched with baseness. A man of such generosity that no day has passed with its sun rising over a head more faithful to its covenant than his. A youth whose judgment in his time has a thousand parts — the smallest fraction of which contains all wisdom combined. Eloquent: whenever he speaks, you find in every word the roots of all brilliance from which excellence branches. His hand is so generous that if a cloud imitated it, no place in the East or West would miss its rain. O prince residing in Manbij, whose ambition is set above the two guardian stars! Is it not wondrous that describing you is beyond reach, and that my imagination limps before your deeds? And that you are in a garment, and your breast within it, though it is wider than the face of the earth? And your heart is in this world — yet if all of mankind and jinn entered it, they would not know how to find their way out. Indeed, every generous man besides you today is worthless, and every praise directed at other than you is wasted.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: last breath, remnant of the soul

context: A major qasida addressed to a patron in Manbij (northern Syria). The poem opens with a ghazal about a phantom visitor who departs too quickly, then transitions to elaborate praise. The closing verse became famous: 'Every generous man besides you is worthless, and every praise for another is wasted.'

titleEn: Grief Agitates and Composure Restrains

verses.ajuz: ما قومه ، ما يومه ، ما المصرع ؟

verses.sadr: لم يرض قلب أبي شجاع مبلغ
ما كان منك إلى خليل قبلها
verses.ajuz: ما يستراب به ولا ما يوجع

verses.sadr: يا من يبدل كل يوم حلة
ما زلت تخلعها على من شاءها
ما زلت تدفع كل أمر فادح
verses.ajuz: حتى أتى الأمر الذي لا يدفع
وجه له من كل قبح برقع

verses.sadr: من كان فيه لكل قوم ملجأ
إن حل في فرس ففيها ربها
أو حل في روم ففيها قيصر
verses.ajuz: أو حل في عرب ففيها تبع

verses.sadr: قد كان أسرع فارس في طعنة

translationEn: Grief agitates and composure restrains, and tears between them are both rebellious and obedient. The two contend over the tears of a sleepless eye — one brings them forth and the other holds them back. Sleep after Abu Shuja' has fled, and the night is blind and the stars are lame. I am a coward before parting from those I love, yet when my soul senses death approaching, I grow brave. Life is sweet only for the ignorant or the heedless, unaware of what has passed and what is yet to come — and for the one who deceives himself about realities and bids his soul pursue the impossible, so it hopes in vain. Traces linger after their owners for a while, then annihilation reaches them and they follow. Where is the one whose building includes the Pyramids? What was his people? What was his day? What was his end? The heart of Abu Shuja' was never satisfied with any attainment before death, nor could any place contain him. We thought his homes were filled with gold, but he died and every dwelling was empty. Cool my burning innards with a word, if you can, for you have the power to harm when you wish and to heal. Never before this did anything come from you to a friend that would arouse suspicion or cause pain. I have always seen you: whenever a calamity struck, a resolute heart drove it away from you. O you who changed your garment every day — how did you accept a garment that cannot be removed? You kept bestowing your robes on whoever desired them until today you wear one you cannot take off. You kept repelling every crushing blow until the blow came that cannot be repelled. Ugliness upon your face, O Time! It is a face veiled with every form of ugliness. Shall the like of Abu Shuja' Fatik die while his envious, bow-legged eunuch rival lives on? You kept the most lying of liars alive and took away the most truthful speaker and listener. You left the foulest, most blameworthy stench and stole the sweetest fragrance that ever wafted. He has departed, and every intimate companion and drinking friend, after devotion, sees him off and bids farewell — he who was a refuge for every people and whose sword had a pasture among every nation. Had he settled among Persians, he would have been their lord Khosrow, before whom necks submit. Had he settled among Romans, he would have been their Caesar; had he settled among Arabs, he would have been their Tubba'. He was the swiftest horseman in a charge — but Death was swifter. May the hands of horsemen never again turn a lance after him, nor four legs carry a steed.
context: One of al-Mutanabbi's greatest elegies, composed on the death of Abu Shuja' Fatik, a military commander. The poem became a masterpiece of Arabic funerary verse. Its philosophical reflections on mortality — 'Life is sweet only for the ignorant' — and the comparison of the dead hero to Khosrow, Caesar, and Tubba' are among the most quoted passages in Arabic literature.

titleEn: By My Father, We Loved and Parted
translationEn: By my father's life — I loved someone and we parted, and God decreed after that a reunion. But we were separated a whole year, and when at last we met, his greeting to me was a farewell.
context: A poignant couplet about the cruelty of time and separation. After a year of longing, the reunion lasts only long enough for a greeting that is simultaneously a farewell. This miniature poem captures al-Mutanabbi's mastery of condensing profound emotion into minimal form.

titleEn: With It and Its Like He Split the Ranks
translationEn: With this sword and its like he split the ranks, and death itself slipped away from its wielder. So leave it fallen, for you are of noble men whose armor is the spearhead and the sword.
context: A short martial poem praising a warrior who splits enemy ranks with his sword. The poet tells him to leave the broken blade behind — men of his caliber wear spears and swords as their armor.

titleEn: A Messenger Claiming Kinship with My Beloved

verses.sadr: فهيج من شوقي وما من مذلة
وكل وداد لا يدوم على الأذى
فإن يكن الفعل الذي ساء واحدا
فإن كان يبغي قتلها يك قاتلا

translationEn: One came to me claiming kinship with the one I love, while arrows from his hands whistled around me. He stirred my longing — and it was no humiliation that I yearned, for the noble soul is one that grows attached. Any love that does not endure through harm as my love for al-Husayn endures is feeble. If the deed that displeased was one, the deeds that brought joy were a thousand. My soul — may my soul be ransom for his — yet some masters are harsh. If he desires to kill it, let him be the one who kills it with his own hands, for a noble death at noble hands is itself noble.
context: Addressed to al-Husayn (likely Abu al-Asha'ir), this poem reveals the complexity of al-Mutanabbi's relationship with his patron. Despite being harmed, the poet's love endures. The final verse is striking: if the patron wishes to kill him, let it be by his own hands — for a noble death is noble.

titleAr: موقع الخيل من نداك طفيف موقع الخيل من نداك طفيف
titleEn: Horses Are a Trifle Beside Your Generosity

verses.sadr: موقع الخيل من نداك طفيف موقع الخيل من نداك طفيف

translationEn: The worth of horses compared to your generosity is trifling, even if the steeds numbered in the thousands. Among words there is a single word that gathers all description — and that is the well-known thoroughbred itself. We have no right to choose what your generosity gives — everything the noble one bestows is noble.
context: A poem thanking a patron for the gift of horses, though al-Mutanabbi insists that even a thousand steeds are a trifle compared to the patron's overflowing generosity. The final verse is a graceful compliment: whatever a noble man gives is inherently noble.

titleEn: I Have Prepared Swords for the Treacherous

verses.sadr: لا يذكر الخير إن ذكرت ولا

translationEn: I have prepared swords for the treacherous, with which I cut off their noses. May God show no mercy to their skulls — I sent their crown-bones flying off their heads. The only thing my sword resents is their small number, wishing hundreds were thousands. O vilest of flesh — I bereaved you of blood and made your bellies a feast for slinking hyenas. I promised this blade to whoever confronted me, and when I confronted you, I feared I would break my promise. When you are mentioned, no good is recalled, and no pair of eyes follows you with longing. When a man frightens me with his treachery, I deliver him to the very fate he feared.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: skull bones, crown of the head

context: A fierce poem of self-praise and threat against treacherous enemies. Al-Mutanabbi revels in graphic martial imagery — severed noses, flying skulls, hyena-food — demonstrating the violent consequences awaiting anyone who betrays him. The poem reflects a dangerous period in his life when he faced betrayal.

titleEn: How Light Is Long Imprisonment and Ruin

verses.sadr: كن أيها السجن كيف شئت فقد

translationEn: How light are long detention and ruin, and prison and chains, O Abu Dalaf! Without choosing, I accepted your charity toward me — for hunger makes lions accept carrion. Be as you wish, O prison, for the soul of one who acknowledges his fate has made its peace with death. If dwelling within you were a disgrace, the pearl would not dwell within the shell.
context: Composed during al-Mutanabbi's imprisonment, likely in the early period of his life when he was captured after his failed revolt claiming prophethood (from which his nickname 'al-Mutanabbi' derives). The final verse became one of the most famous lines in Arabic: 'If dwelling in you were a disgrace, the pearl would not dwell in the shell.'

titleEn: Sleeplessness Upon Sleeplessness

verses.sadr: جهد الصبابة أن تكون كما أرى
جربت من نار الهوى ما تنطفي

verses.ajuz: فعجبت كيف يموت من لا يعشق

verses.sadr: نبكي على الدنيا وما من معشر

verses.ajuz: كنزوا الكنوز فما بقين ولا بقوا

verses.sadr: أما بنو أوس بن معن الرضا

translationEn: Sleeplessness upon sleeplessness — and one like me has reason to lie awake — with anguish ever increasing and tears brimming. The utmost of passion is to be as you see: a sleepless eye and a pounding heart. Whenever lightning flashes or a bird sings, I turn with a heart full of longing. I have known a fire of love beside which the fire of brushwood is extinguished and falls short of what it burns. I used to blame the people of love until I tasted it myself, and then I wondered: how does one who does not love even stay alive? I excused them and knew my sin — that I had mocked them, only to meet the same fate they met. O sons of our father, we are the people of dwellings where the crow of parting forever croaks. We weep over this world, yet there is no company the world has gathered that did not scatter. Where are the mighty Caesars who hoarded their treasures? Neither their treasures remained nor they. Death is coming, and souls are precious, and the fool is the one deluded by what he possesses. Man goes on hoping while life is sweet, and old age is more dignified while youth is more reckless. I wept over youth while my hair was still black and my face still had its luster — fearing for it before the day of its departure — until I nearly choked on my own tears. As for the sons of Aws ibn Ma'n — their fragrance is of musk, except that I marveled at their homes: suns rose from them though the East was not there. I wondered at a land over which the clouds of their hands rain, yet its rocks do not sprout leaves. The Most Merciful has not created anyone like Muhammad, and I believe He never will. Rain upon me the overflowing cloud of your generosity, and look upon me with mercy lest I drown. He lies — the son of a base woman — who says in his ignorance: 'The generous have died, and you are still alive and provided for.'
context: One of al-Mutanabbi's most celebrated poems, blending love, philosophy, and panegyric. The wisdom verses on mortality — 'Where are the mighty Caesars who hoarded treasures?' and 'We weep over the world yet no company the world gathered failed to scatter' — became proverbial. The line 'I wondered how one who does not love stays alive' is among the most quoted in Arabic love poetry. The poem ends praising Muhammad ibn Aws.

titleEn: What Station Shall I Ascend?
translationEn: What station shall I ascend to? What great one shall I fear? Everything that God has created, and everything He has not, is contemptible in my ambition — like a single hair on the parting of my head.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: parting of the hair, crown of the head

context: Perhaps the most concentrated expression of al-Mutanabbi's legendary self-regard. In just three short verses, he declares that all of creation — and even what has not been created — is beneath his ambition. This poem has been both celebrated and criticized for its extreme audacity.

titleAr: لعينيك ما يلقى الفؤاد وما لقي
titleEn: For Your Eyes Is What the Heart Endures

verses.sadr: لعينيك ما يلقى الفؤاد وما لقي
verses.ajuz: وللحب ما لم يبق مني وما بقي

verses.sadr: وما كنت ممن يدخل العشق قلبه
وأحلى الهوى ما شك في الوصل ربه
verses.ajuz: وفي الهجر فهو الدهر يرجو ويتقي

verses.sadr: وغضبى من الإدلال سكرى من الصبى
وما كل من يهوى يعف إذا خلا
سقى الله أيام الصبا ما يسرها

translationEn: For your eyes is what the heart endures and has endured, and for love is what remains of me and what is gone. I was never one whose heart was entered by passion, but whoever beholds your eyelids falls in love. Between contentment and anger, nearness and distance, there is room for the tears of a brimming eye. The sweetest love is that which keeps its owner in doubt — of union and of separation — so he forever hopes and fears. Angry from coquetry, drunk with youth — I interceded with her through the freshness of my youth. A mouth sweet-toothed and honey-fronted, bright: I hid my mouth from it, and so it kissed the crown of my head. Necks of gazelles like your neck visited me, and I could not tell the bare-necked from the bejeweled. Not everyone who loves remains chaste when alone, as I do, and satisfies love while the cavalry charges. May God water the days of youth with what delights them, with the effect of aged Babylonian wine.
context: A pure ghazal (love poem) in which al-Mutanabbi describes the exquisite torment of love. The verse 'The sweetest love is that which keeps its owner in doubt' is a celebrated observation on the psychology of desire. Unusually for al-Mutanabbi, this poem is entirely devoted to love without transitioning to praise or self-glorification.

titleEn: A Braided One Without Flaw
translationEn: A braided one without any flaw, except that she is not fit for an embrace. When she departs, it is not by choice, and when she visits, it is not from longing. She was commanded to be lifted away, and she parted from us without any grief at the event of separation.
context: A clever riddle-poem. The 'braided one' appears at first to be a woman, but the clues reveal she is actually a tent or a flag — something with 'braids' (ropes) that is erected and taken down on command, feels no longing, and cannot be embraced. This shows al-Mutanabbi's wit and mastery of double meaning.

titleEn: What Ails the Green Meadows and Gardens?

verses.sadr: ثم مضى لا عاد من مفارق

translationEn: What ails the green meadows and gardens, whose open spaces complain of too many obstacles? Snow settled upon them like elbows propped, freezing the spittle above the teeth of one who spits. Then it departed — may it never return from its departure — led by its own melting and driven from behind. The melting patches were like a searching fugitive, eating from short, clinging vegetation — like scraping ink from parchments. I hunt it on a horse like a falcon: free-strided on the right, long in the poll, thick-legged with close-set elbows, broad-chested with rising veins, wide-nostriled and lean-flanked, white-stockinged, full-bodied, dark bay, spirited — his blaze like the rising sun.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: blaze, white mark on a horse's forehead

context: A vivid nature-and-horse description poem in rajaz meter. Al-Mutanabbi describes the melting of snow from green meadows, comparing the retreating patches to a fleeing fugitive. He then gives a masterful anatomical portrait of his hunting horse, demonstrating the Arabic poetic tradition of wasf al-khayl (horse description).

titleEn: If He Excelled in Praising Her

verses.ajuz: لقد فاته الحسن في الوصف لك
ت يبقى لديك ولا ما ملك

translationEn: If another poet excelled in describing her beauty, he nonetheless fell short of capturing your splendor. For you are an ocean, and the seas themselves would disdain to be compared to these mere ponds. You are like your own sword — neither what you possess endures with you nor what others possess. What you have bestowed exceeds the flow of rivers, and what you have shed surpasses their waters. You have wielded both harm and grace from a position of power, revolving over mankind like the celestial sphere.
context: A short panegyric in the mutaqarib meter comparing the patron to a boundless ocean. Al-Mutanabbi contrasts the patron's generosity and martial power through images of flowing water and spilled blood, culminating in the cosmic metaphor of the revolving heavens.

titleEn: I Have Not Seen a Companion but You
translationEn: I have not found among all my companions anyone but you, and that is not for any reason other than your sincere affection for me. Nor is it for the sake of others' love, but rather because I have come to both hope in you and fear you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I kept company with, I sat with
I have come to be (in the evening)

context: A brief two-verse fragment expressing devotion to a patron. The duality of hope and fear in the final hemistich is a hallmark of Al-Mutanabbi's complex relationship with his patrons, reflecting both reverence and dependency.

titleAr: فدى لك من يقصر عن مداكا
titleEn: May All Who Fall Short of You Be Your Ransom

verses.sadr: فدى لك من يقصر عن مداكا
ولو قلنا فدى لك من يساوي

verses.ajuz: وها أنا ما ضربت وقد أحاكا

verses.sadr: وما أنا غير سهم في هواء
حيي من إل هي أن يراني

translationEn: May all who fall short of your heights be your ransom — then every king would be sacrificed for you. And had we said 'may those who equal you be your ransom,' we would have prayed for the survival of your enemies. I depart having sealed my heart with your love, forbidding anyone else to dwell therein. You have burdened me with such immense gratitude that I can scarcely move under its weight. I fear it may prove too heavy for the camels, so they would refuse to carry us anywhere but toward you. Perhaps God will make this journey a means to settle permanently under your protection. If I could, I would lower my gaze and not look upon anything until I see you again. How can I be patient without you, when your overflowing generosity has sufficed me while nothing suffices you? Will you leave me when the very sun is my sandal, and my walking in it wears through the strap? My sorrow is fierce though we have not yet departed — how will it be when the journey becomes a race? This longing before parting is already a sword, and here I am wounded though it has not yet struck. When farewell approaches, my heart tells my mouth: be silent, may I never be your companion again. How many a grieving soul lies between here and al-Thawiya, to whom my arrival says: this for that. How the ears are delighted, not knowing whether to marvel more at my praise or at your eminence. Noble one whose virtues mirror his father's — your sons will one day meet their grandfather's qualities in you. Among lovers there is one uniquely stricken with passion, while another merely claims to share it. When tears mingle upon cheeks, you can tell who truly weeps from who merely pretends. Who could replace you once we part, when all people besides you are but visitors? I am nothing but an arrow in the air, returning without finding anything to hold onto. My God would be ashamed to see me having left your dwelling after He chose you.
context: A magnificent farewell qasida addressed to a patron, likely Sayf al-Dawla. The 20-verse poem is one of Al-Mutanabbi's most emotionally charged, blending panegyric with genuine sorrow at parting. The famous verse 'when tears mingle upon cheeks, you can tell who truly weeps from who merely pretends' became a proverbial saying in Arabic.

titleAr: قد بلغت الذي أردت من البر
titleEn: You Have Achieved the Piety You Sought

verses.sadr: قد بلغت الذي أردت من البر
وإذا لم تسر إلى الدار في وق
verses.ajuz: تك ذا خفت أن تسير إليكا

translationEn: You have attained the righteousness you sought, and what this noble one deserves from you. And if you do not go to his house at a time of your choosing, I fear his house will come to you.
context: A brief two-verse fragment in the khafif meter, urging someone to visit a noble patron. The subtle warning in the second verse suggests that failing to visit the patron willingly may result in being summoned — a diplomatic nudge characteristic of court poetry.

titleEn: Ignorance Killed You Before Your Death

verses.ajuz: لما صرتم نسل الذي ماله نسل

translationEn: Ignorance has killed you before your actual death, and your lightness has made even ants drag you along. You are the son of Abu al-Tayyib's dog — what is wrong with you? You grasped at claims though you have no sense. Had my catapults struck you and your stock were strong, you would have crumbled — so how when you have no stock at all? And had you been people of sound judgment, you would not have become the offspring of one who has no offspring worth mentioning.
context: A scathing satirical attack in the tawil meter. Al-Mutanabbi was famous for his devastating hija (invective), and here he denies his enemies any claim to intelligence, lineage, or significance, comparing them to creatures so insignificant that ants could drag them.

titleAr: لا خيل عندك تهديها ولا مال
titleEn: You Have No Horses to Gift, Nor Wealth

verses.sadr: لا خيل عندك تهديها ولا مال
verses.ajuz: فليسعد النطق إن لم تسعد الحال

verses.sadr: لا يدرك المجد إلا سيد فطن
القاتل السيف في جسم القتيل به
له من الوحش ما اختارت أسنته
لا يعرف الرزء في مال ولا ولد
لا يحرم البعد أهل البعد نائله

verses.ajuz: فما الذي بتوقي ما أتى نالوا
في الحمد حاء ولا ميم ولا دال

translationEn: You have no horses to gift nor wealth, so let speech be fortunate if fortune fails. Reward the prince whose blessings come as surprises without words, while the favors of others are mere talk. Perhaps a shy maiden of the tribe, languid and bashful, has repaid kindness to its bestower. Though the tight-woven forms of poetry deny me their backs to ride, I still have among them a neighing voice. I did not give thanks because wealth delighted me — abundance and scarcity are the same to me. None attains glory but a lord perceptive of what is hard for lords to accomplish. The spear, when it quivers in his palm, knows that horses and heroes will suffer by it. I said 'like you' — but even the letter 'like' diminishes: I said 'like the sun,' yet the sun has no equal. The sword that kills dwells in the body of the slain, for swords too, like men, have appointed terms. From the wild beasts his lance-tips select what they choose — wild asses, ostriches, gazelles, and wolves. He knows no loss in wealth or children except when departing guests cause him grief. His swords feed the hours fresh blood, as if every hour brings a battle and a return. Distance does not deprive the distant of his bounty, nor do little children lack the strength to reach it. His envier may call him mad, for when things are confused, some wisdom becomes a shackle. His is the highest nobility that precedes him — those who tried to avoid what came could not escape. Abu Shuja, father of all the brave, a terror nurtured by the terrors of war. He has monopolized all praise until no boaster has even the letters of 'praise' left. You refined your judgment in my care and honor — the noble one devises ways to reach the heights. Until you became one whose news travels everywhere, and the very stars in your hands have hopes. My praise has been elongated by the stature of its wearer, for praise of a short man comes out short. A person can only reach his capacity — not every beast of burden gallops under the saddle.
context: One of Al-Mutanabbi's grand panegyric qasidas in the basit meter. The famous opening 'You have no horses to gift nor wealth, so let speech be fortunate' is among the most cited lines in Arabic poetry. The poem was likely composed for a patron early in his career, before he achieved wealth himself. The closing proverb about every creature having its own capacity became widely quoted.

titleEn: I Would Ransom — What Pleases the Messenger?
translationEn: May I be your ransom — what is it that pleases the messenger? You are the healthy one in this matter, not the ailing. The consequences of this will harm the enemy and remain fixed among them, while this other thing will pass away.
context: A brief two-verse piece in the mutaqarib meter, apparently a fragment from a larger occasion. Al-Mutanabbi reassures the patron that his enemies' temporary gains will fade, while the lasting consequences will remain against them.

titleAr: لك يا منازل في القلوب منازل
titleEn: You, O Dwellings, Hold Dwellings in Our Hearts

verses.sadr: لك يا منازل في القلوب منازل
حتى أبو الفضل ابن عبدالله رؤ
يدري بما بك قبل تظهره له
لو طاب مولد كل حي مثله
جفخت وهم لا يجفخون بها بهم
يا فخر فإن الناس فيك ثلاثة
أثني عليك ولو تشاء لقلت لي

verses.ajuz: من جوده في كل فج وابل

verses.sadr: من لي بفهم أهيل عصر يدعي

translationEn: You, O abandoned dwellings, hold dwellings in our hearts — you are desolate, yet our hearts are inhabited by you. They know this though I did not inform them, for the wise man weeps first over what he knows. I am he whose glance brought on his own death — so who is to blame when the slain is also the slayer? How many a station burned me with longing after the watcher grew suspicious and the reproacher persisted. Enjoy and take pleasure, for all things have endings whenever they have beginnings. Moments of joy pass by like farewell kisses bestowed by a departing beloved. Time has bolted wildly — no pleasure is pure from what taints it, nor is any happiness complete. Until Abu al-Fadl ibn Abdullah — beholding him is the fulfillment of wishes, yet it is an awe-inspiring station. Veiled behind a pavilion of reverence that turns back the reins while the camels are still running. In him are the qualities of the sun, the clouds, the seas, the lions, and the winds. With him are two intellects, refined learning, the wellspring of life, and the sources of death. He knows what ails you before you reveal it, from his own perception, and answers before you ask. His words are swords that cut through — every obstacle falls apart beneath them. His noble deeds have defeated all noble deeds until virtues seem like mere clusters of grapes. If every person were born as nobly as he, women would give birth without needing midwives. Let the sons of al-Hasan increase in humility — far be it that torches can be hidden in darkness. Their qualities boast for them though they do not boast — such traits are proof of radiant lineage. O Pride! People regarding you are only three: one who marvels, one who envies, or one who is ignorant. You have risen so high that you do not care, once they have known you, whether the speaker praises or blames. I praise you, and had you wished you could say to me 'you fell short' — withholding from me would itself be a gift. The eloquent dare not recite even a verse here, but I am the bold lion. All the poets of the Age of Ignorance never achieved my poetry, nor has Babylon heard of my sorcery. And if my blame comes to you from a lesser man — from his generosity in every mountain pass flows a torrent. Who will grant me understanding from the people of this age, who claim that among them a mute fool can be counted as an Indian sage?
context: A grand qasida of 24 verses in the kamil meter, addressed to Abu al-Fadl ibn Abdullah. The poem follows the classical structure: nasib (nostalgic opening), ghazal (love passage), and madih (panegyric). Al-Mutanabbi's signature fakhr (self-praise) emerges powerfully in the closing verses where he declares his superiority over all poets of the Jahiliyya. The line 'people regarding you are only three' became a famous proverb.

titleEn: O You Who Humbles the Lion with His Whip
translationEn: O you who humbles the mighty lion with just your whip — for whom then have you saved the polished sword? A calamity fell upon the Jordan from that lion, piling the skulls of companions into mounds. A beast that, when it comes to the lake to drink, its roar reaches the Euphrates and the Nile. Dyed with the blood of horsemen, wearing within its thicket a second thicket from its twin manes. Whenever its eyes were met, they seemed to be the campfire of a tribe blazing beneath the darkness. Living in the solitude of monks, except that it knows neither the forbidden nor the permitted. It treads the earth gently out of its own pride, like a physician feeling the pulse of the sick. It dropped its prey and snarled over it — and you approached so close it mistook you for a scrounger. So the two creatures resembled each other in their courage, but differed in their willingness to share the meal.
context: A vivid description poem (wasf) of a lion hunt, praising the patron's courage in confronting a lion by the Jordan River. The poem brilliantly humanizes the lion — comparing its solitude to monks, its gait to a physician — before the dramatic climax where the patron approaches so boldly that the lion mistakes him for a fellow predator. This was likely composed for Sayf al-Dawla after a hunting expedition.

titleAr: أحيا وأيسر ما قاسيت ما قتلا
titleEn: I Live, Though the Least of What I Suffered Would Have Killed

verses.sadr: أحيا وأيسر ما قاسيت ما قتلا
verses.ajuz: والبين جار على ضعفي وما عدلا

verses.sadr: والوجد يقوى كما تقوى النوى أبدا
verses.ajuz: والصبر ينحل في جسمي كما نحلا

verses.sadr: بما بجفنيك من سحر صلي دنفا
verses.ajuz: يهوى الحياة وأما إن صددت فلا

verses.sadr: إلا يشب فلقد شابت له كبد

verses.ajuz: تزوره من رياح الشرق ما عقلا

verses.sadr: ها فانظري أو فظني بي تري حرقا
verses.ajuz: من لم يذق طرفا منها فقده وألا

verses.sadr: عل الأمير يرى ذلي فيشفع لي
verses.ajuz: إلى التي تركتني في الهوى مثلا

translationEn: I live, though the least of what I suffered would have killed, and separation has oppressed my weakness unjustly. Longing grows stronger as distance ever grows, and patience dissolves in my body as it wastes away. Were it not for parting from loved ones, death would never have found paths to our souls. With the sorcery in your eyelids, pray over a lovesick man who desires life — but if you turn away, then no. Though his hair has not greyed, his liver has grown grey with a greyness that, if dyed with consolation, would fade. He yearns with such longing that were it not for a breeze that visits him from the eastern winds, he would lose his mind. Look, or merely imagine me, and you will see a burning — whoever has not tasted a portion of it is lost and bewildered. Perhaps the prince will see my abasement and intercede for me with her who has made me a proverb in love.
context: A ghazal (love poem) in the basit meter combining passionate complaint with a closing appeal to the patron. The famous third verse — 'Were it not for parting from loved ones, death would never have found paths to our souls' — became one of the most quoted lines in Arabic love poetry, expressing the idea that heartbreak is the gateway through which mortality reaches us.

titleAr: إذا ما شربت الخمر صرفا مهنأ
titleEn: When You Drink Wine Pure and Pleasant

verses.sadr: إذا ما شربت الخمر صرفا مهنأ
verses.ajuz: شربنا الذي من مثله شرب الكرم

verses.sadr: ألا حبذا قوم نداما هم القنا

translationEn: When you drink wine pure and pleasant, we drink that from which the vine itself first drank. How excellent are companions who are like spears — they pour it freely, and their cupbearer is resolve itself.
context: A brief two-verse poem that transforms a wine-drinking scene into a martial metaphor. While the patron drinks actual wine, the poet and his warrior companions drink from the same vine of courage — their cupbearer is sheer determination. This blending of the khamriyya (wine poem) with martial imagery is characteristic of Al-Mutanabbi.

titleEn: O the Burning of My Heart for One Whose Heart Is Cold

verses.sadr: مالي أكتم حبا قد برى جسدي

verses.ajuz: وكان أحسن ما في الأحسن الشيم
يا أعدل الناس إلا في معاملتي

verses.sadr: أن تحسب الشحم فيمن شحمه ورم
بأنني خير من تسقى به قدم
أنا الذي نظر الأعمى إلى أدبي
يا من يعز علينا أن نفارقهم

verses.ajuz: لو أن أمركم من أمرنا أمم

verses.sadr: إن كان سركم ما قال حاسدنا
شر البلاد مكان لا صديق به
verses.ajuz: وشر ما يكسب الإنسان ما يصم
قد ضمن الدر إلا أنه كلم

translationEn: O the burning of my heart for one whose heart is cold, and who sees illness in my body and my state! Why should I conceal a love that has wasted my body, while whole nations claim love for Sayf al-Dawla? If love for his radiant face unites us, then I wish we could be apportioned shares according to our love. I visited him when the Indian swords were sheathed, and I looked upon him when the swords were streaming blood — and he was the most beautiful of all God's creation, and the best thing in the best of men was his character. Every time you rout an army and it flees, your ambitions drive you to pursue its tracks. O most just of people — except in your treatment of me — for I know that he is but one of mankind. My dispute is with you, and you are both the adversary and the judge. I seek refuge for your truthful glances from mistaking swelling for fatness in one whose fat is merely a tumor. And what use is a man's eyesight in this world if lights and darkness appear equal to him? The assembly will come to know, among all gathered in our council, that I am the best foot upon which the earth is trodden. I am he whose literature the blind can see, and whose words reach the ears of the deaf. I sleep with my eyes full of rest while others stay awake for my verses, quarreling and disputing over them. And a fool whom my laughter encouraged in his ignorance — until a hand and mouth of keen perception reached him. When you see the lion's fangs bared, do not suppose the lion is smiling. The horse and the night and the desert know me, and the sword and the lance and the paper and the pen. O you whom it grieves us to part from — finding everything after you is nothingness. How worthy we were of your honor, if only your concern were close to ours. If what our envier said pleased you, then a wound that satisfies you causes me no pain. How often you seek a flaw in us and it defeats you — and God and nobility despise what you attempt. Farthest from flaw and deficiency is my honor — I am the Pleiades, and those two are old age and decrepitude. The worst of lands is a place with no friend in it, and the worst thing a man earns is what disgraces him. This is my reproach of you — except that it is affection: it contains pearls, except that they are wounds.
context: This is Al-Mutanabbi's single most celebrated poem, the legendary qasida addressed to Sayf al-Dawla al-Hamdani in Aleppo. Composed around 345 AH when tensions grew between poet and patron, it is both an adoring tribute and a bitter reproach. It contains the most famous lines in all of Arabic poetry: 'The horse and the night and the desert know me,' 'I am he whose literature the blind can see,' and 'I sleep soundly while creation quarrels over my verses.' The closing line masterfully balances blame with love — his words are pearls, but they wound.

titleEn: Is There No Generous Soul in This World?

verses.sadr: فهل من عاذر في ذا وفي ذا

translationEn: Is there no generous soul in this world who could relieve the heart of its sorrows? Is there no place in this world whose people would delight a resident neighbor? I ended up in the land of Egypt among slaves, where the free man is like an orphan among them. The dark-maned lion among them is like a crow surrounded by vultures and owls. I took to praising him and found it absurd — like calling a halfwit 'O wise one!' And when I turned to satire, I found it futile — like telling a jackal 'O base one!' So is there anyone who would excuse me in both cases? For the sick one is driven toward sickness. When insult comes from a lowly person and I cannot blame the offender, then whom shall I blame?
context: A satirical poem directed at the court of Kafur al-Ikhshidi in Egypt. After Al-Mutanabbi's departure from Sayf al-Dawla's court in Aleppo, he went to Egypt hoping for patronage from Kafur, the Abyssinian former slave who ruled Egypt. Deeply disappointed, he composed devastating satires attacking both Kafur and the entire Egyptian court, expressing contempt at being among people he considered unworthy of either his praise or his blame.

titleEn: Does the Wind Pass Gently at My Command?
translationEn: Does the wind pass gently at my command, and do the clouds travel whenever I wish? No — but the clouds have an inherent nature that causes them to pour forth, and so too do the generous.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: cause it to gush forth, burst

context: A concise two-verse wisdom poem comparing natural generosity to the clouds. Al-Mutanabbi argues that true generosity, like rain, flows from inherent nature rather than external command — a philosophical point that elevates the patron's giving above mere obligation.

titleAr: على قدر أهل العزم تأتي العزائم
titleEn: Great Deeds Come to Match the Greatness of Men

verses.sadr: على قدر أهل العزم تأتي العزائم
ويطلب عند الناس ما عند نفسه
فلم يبق إلا صارم أو ضبارم
إذا برقوا لم تعرف البيض منهم
تقطع ما لا يقطع الدرع والقنا
verses.ajuz: وفر من الفرسان من لا يصادم

verses.sadr: وقفت وما في الموت شك لواقف
verses.ajuz: كأنك في جفن الردى وهو نائم
إلى قول قوم أنت بالغيب عالم

verses.sadr: ألا أيها السيف الذي ليس مغمدا
verses.ajuz: ولا فيه مرتاب ولا منه عاصم

translationEn: Great deeds come to match the greatness of men, and noble acts come to match the measure of the noble. Small things appear great in the eyes of the small, and great things appear small in the eyes of the great. Sayf al-Dawla burdens his army with his own ambition, though the mightiest armies have failed at what he attempts. He demands from people what lies within himself — and that is something not even lions dare claim. The longest-lived birds sacrifice their weapons for him — eagles, both young and ancient vultures. Nothing remained but a sharp sword or a broad blade, for his swords and their hilts were worn through. Does the red fortress of al-Hadath know its own origin, and know which of the two clouds watered it? White clouds watered it before his descent, but when he drew near, skulls watered it instead. He built it high while spears clashed against spears, and waves of death surged around it. It had been afflicted as if by madness, but then it found its amulets in the corpses of the slain upon it. They came to you dragging iron armor as though they rode upon steeds with no legs. When they flashed their swords, you could not tell the white blades from their garments and turbans, which were of the same steel. An army whose advance fills the east and west of the earth, whose murmuring reaches the ear of Gemini. Every tongue and nation gathered in it, so that its events could only be understood through interpreters. It cuts what neither armor nor spears can cut, and among its horsemen fled those who cannot be confronted. You stood where there is no doubt of death for one who stands — as if you were in the eyelid of destruction while it slept. The heroes pass by you wounded in defeat, while your face is radiant and your mouth is smiling. Does one deny the scent of the lion until he tastes it? As for those who say you know the unseen — you scattered them across all of al-Uhaydib like coins scattered over a bride. O sword that is never sheathed, about which there is no doubt, and from which there is no protection — congratulations to the striking of heads, to glory and eminence, to those who hope in you, and to Islam, that you are safe. They contested it while fates were judges, and his splitting of enemy skulls through you is everlasting.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Hadath, a fortress on the Byzantine frontier

context: The celebrated 'Hadathiyya' — Al-Mutanabbi's greatest battle qasida, composed in 954 CE after Sayf al-Dawla recaptured the frontier fortress of al-Hadath al-Hamra from the Byzantines and rebuilt it. The opening couplet became the most famous wisdom verse in Arabic: 'Great deeds come to match the greatness of men.' The vivid battle descriptions — armies dragging iron like legless steeds, skulls watering the fortress instead of rain — represent the pinnacle of Arabic martial poetry. The verse 'You stood where there is no doubt of death... as if you were in the eyelid of destruction while it slept' is considered one of the supreme achievements of Arabic poetic imagery.

titleAr: بم التعلل لا أهل ولا وطن
titleEn: With What Shall I Console Myself? No Family, No Homeland

verses.sadr: بم التعلل لا أهل ولا وطن
verses.ajuz: ولا نديم ولا كأس ولا سكن

verses.sadr: أريد من زمني ذا أن يبلغني
verses.ajuz: ما ليس يبلغه من نفسه الزمن

verses.sadr: لا تلق دهرك إلا غير مكترث
فما يديم سرور ما سررت به

verses.ajuz: هووا وما عرفوا الدنيا وما فطنوا
في إثر كل قبيح وجهه حسن

verses.sadr: يا من نعيت على بعد بمجلسه
كم قد قتلت وكم قد مت عندكم
قد كان شاهد دفني قبل قولهم
verses.ajuz: جماعة ثم ماتوا قبل من دفنوا

verses.sadr: ما كل ما يتمنى المرء يدركه
verses.ajuz: تجري الرياح بما لا تشتهي السفن

verses.sadr: إني أصاحب حلمي وهو بي كرم
verses.ajuz: ولا أصاحب حلمي وهو بي جبن

translationEn: With what shall I console myself — no family, no homeland, no companion, no cup, no dwelling? I want from this age of mine what even time itself cannot achieve. Do not meet your fate except with indifference, so long as your soul accompanies your body. For no joy you experienced will last forever, nor will grief restore what has been lost. What has harmed lovers most is that they loved without understanding the world or being perceptive. Their eyes spend themselves in tears and their souls chase after every ugly one whose face appears beautiful. O you for whom my death was announced in his distant assembly — everyone is held hostage by what the eulogists claim. How many times I was killed, how many times I died among you — then I shook it off, and the grave and the shroud vanished. There were witnesses to my burial before their claims — a group who then died before the one they buried. Not everything a man wishes for does he attain — the winds blow contrary to what the ships desire. The reward of every one close to you is boredom, and the lot of every lover among you is rancor. I keep company with my forbearance when it is nobility in me, but I do not keep company with forbearance when it is cowardice. I stayed awake after my departure in loneliness for you, then my bitterness hardened and sleep returned. And if I am afflicted with affection like yours, then I deserve a parting like it.
context: One of Al-Mutanabbi's most philosophically rich qasidas, composed during his period of wandering and disillusionment. The opening captures his existential solitude — stripped of home, family, and companionship. The poem contains one of the most famous proverbs in all of Arabic: 'Not everything a man wishes for does he attain — the winds blow contrary to what the ships desire.' The distinction between forbearance-as-nobility and forbearance-as-cowardice reflects his fierce sense of personal honor.

titleEn: People Before Us Kept Company with This Age

verses.sadr: وإذا لم يكن من الموت بد

translationEn: People before us kept company with this age, and what concerned them of its nature concerns us too. They all departed from it with a choking grief, even if it gladdened some of them at times. Whenever time grows a reed, man mounts a spearhead upon that reed. What the souls desire is too small a thing for people to feud over and destroy one another. Yet the young man meets death frowning and does not meet humiliation. And if life were to remain for anyone living, we would count the bravest among us as the most misguided. And when there is no escape from death, it is weakness to be a coward.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a choking grief, a lump in the throat

context: One of Al-Mutanabbi's most famous wisdom poems, often recited as a meditation on mortality and courage. The final verse — 'When there is no escape from death, it is weakness to be a coward' — became one of the most quoted lines in Arabic culture, appearing in everything from political speeches to everyday conversation. The poem was likely composed during his travels through the desert, reflecting on the universal human condition.

titleAr: أبلى الهوى أسفا يوم النوى بدني
titleEn: Love Has Worn My Body with Sorrow on the Day of Parting

verses.sadr: أبلى الهوى أسفا يوم النوى بدني
روح تردد في مثل الخلال إذا
verses.ajuz: أطارت الريح عنه الثوب لم يبن

translationEn: Love has worn my body with grief on the day of parting, and separation has divorced my eyelids from sleep. A soul that flutters within a body thin as a toothpick — if the wind blew the garment from it, nothing would be seen. It is enough to show my emaciation that I am a man whom, were it not for my speaking to you, you would not see at all.
context: A brief but intensely vivid ghazal fragment. The image of the lover's body being thinner than a toothpick — invisible if not for his voice — is a masterful hyperbole in the Arabic love poetry tradition. Al-Mutanabbi pushes the conventional trope of the wasted lover's body to its logical extreme: he exists only as a voice.

titleEn: I Concealed My Love Even from You, Out of Dignity

verses.sadr: كأنه زاد حتى فاض عن جسدي
verses.ajuz: فصار سقمي به في جسم كتماني

translationEn: I concealed my love even from you, out of dignity, until concealing it and revealing it became equal. It was as though it increased until it overflowed beyond my body, and my illness from it transferred to the body of my concealment itself.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: out of honor, out of dignity

context: A delicate two-verse ghazal about love so overwhelming that it can no longer be concealed. The paradox of love sickening even the act of concealment itself is a sophisticated poetic conceit. The brevity of the poem amplifies its emotional intensity.

titleEn: Sound Judgment Comes Before the Courage of the Brave
translationEn: Sound judgment comes before the courage of the brave — it holds first place, and courage holds the second. When both unite in a free soul, that soul attains every station of glory. Often a young man has pierced his peers with judgment before the peers engage in combat with lances. Were it not for intellect, the lowliest lion would be closer to honor than any human being.
context: One of Al-Mutanabbi's most celebrated wisdom poems, frequently quoted in Arabic discourse on leadership. The argument that intellect surpasses raw courage — and that without reason, even a lion would outrank a human — became a foundational maxim in Arabic political and military thought. The poem is often cited in discussions of strategy versus brute force.

titleEn: People Are Alike Until They See You

verses.sadr: إن كان فيما نراه من كرم

translationEn: People, until they see you, are all alike — and time is but a word, and you are its meaning. Generosity is an eye, and you are its pupil; valor is an arm, and you are its right hand. O departing one — everyone who bids you farewell bids farewell to his religion and his worldly life alike. If there is any increase possible in the generosity we see in you, may God increase it for you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its pupil (of the eye), its gazer

context: A farewell panegyric of exquisite concision. In just four verses, Al-Mutanabbi declares the patron to be the meaning of time itself, the pupil of generosity's eye, and the right hand of valor. The farewell verse is especially poignant — those who bid him goodbye lose both their worldly fortune and their spiritual guidance. This economy of expression shows Al-Mutanabbi at his most brilliant.

titleEn: If Tayy Were Indeed Base
translationEn: If the tribe of Tayy were indeed base, then the basest among them is Rabi'a and his sons. And if Tayy were noble, then Wardan's father must belong to some other tribe. We passed by a slave of his in Hisma whose nostrils and mouth exuded baseness. He drove my servants away along with his wife, destroying them and what wealth I had with them. But if my steeds suffered at their hands, then faces have surely suffered at the edge of my sword.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Hisma, a desert region in northern Arabia

context: A satirical poem born from a personal conflict during Al-Mutanabbi's desert travels. While passing through the Hisma desert, he clashed with members of the tribe of Tayy. The poem attacks a specific individual and his clan, using the classic hija technique of questioning their lineage — if the tribe is noble, this family cannot truly belong to it; if the tribe is base, they are the worst of it.

titleEn: Alas — a Substitute for My Sighing 'Ah!'
translationEn: Alas — a substitute for my sighing 'Ah!' — for one who has departed, and my substitute is remembering her. Or rather for one whose beauty I cannot see, for the origin of 'Ah!' and 'Alas!' is beholding her. A Syrian woman with whom I often sat alone, seeing her face reflected in my eyes. She kissed my eyes, fooling me — for in truth she kissed her own reflection in them. Every wound may hope for healing, but would that it remained forever her dwelling. My cheek grows wet whenever she smiles, from the rain whose lightning is her teeth. We met while the caravan was moving — and what sweetness it was that we recall. I love the land from Homs to Khanasira, and every soul loves where it came alive. Where her cheek met the apples of Lebanon, and my lips met her wine. He made it into mouths for wine-drinking — his east and west submit to him. I have seen all the kings without exception — how can one whose excess is so apparent be concealed? Abu Shuja, the Persian, Adud al-Dawla Fana Khusraw, King of Kings. The world and its children and all it contains are the most precious and exalted of his possessions. Wine finds no flaw in his noble virtues — if he becomes intoxicated, it is a quality he amends. His innate generosity accompanies the wine, and the wine falls short of even his least quality. His crowns shine with his radiance, as his words shine with their meaning. Ambitions gathered in his heart, any single one of which would fill the heart of an entire age. The horseman against whom weapons are wielded — battle and its cavalry praise him. Even if a raid we undertook were mentioned, no commander could boast of it but he. If all the world denied his bounty, his soul would not depart from its own virtues. Like the sun, he does not seek from what he does any recognition among people, nor status. People are like worshippers of idols, but his servant is like one who worships God alone.
context: A grand qasida in the munsarih meter, beginning with a love prelude about a Syrian woman before pivoting to praise of Adud al-Dawla, the powerful Buyid ruler. The closing comparison — ordinary people worship rulers like idol-worshippers, but the true servant serves him as a monotheist serves God — is a bold theological metaphor typical of Al-Mutanabbi's fearless imagery. This poem was composed during his time in Persia.

titleAr: كفى بك داء أن ترى الموت شافيا
titleEn: It Is Illness Enough That You See Death as a Cure

verses.sadr: كفى بك داء أن ترى الموت شافيا
إذا كنت ترضى أن تعيش بذلة
حببتك قلبي قبل حبك من نأى
verses.ajuz: وقد كان غدارا فكن أنت وافيا
رأيتك تصفي الود من ليس صافيا
فإن لم تبد منهم أباد الأعاديا

verses.sadr: أبا المسك ذا الوجه الذي كنت تائقا
verses.ajuz: إليه وذا اليوم الذي كنت راجيا

translationEn: It is illness enough that you see death as a cure, and it suffices for death that it becomes your wish. You wished for it when you wished to find a true friend but were defeated, or an honest enemy but found only a dissembler. If you are content to live in abasement, then do not prepare the Yemeni sword. I have given you, my heart, to love before you loved the one who departed — he was treacherous, so be you faithful. Be less eager, O my heart — for often I have seen you give pure affection to one who is not pure. Those who make for Kafur find all others trivial beside him, for whoever seeks the sea disdains the rivulets. He destroys the enmities of aggressors with his kindness; and if they do not perish, he destroys the enemies themselves. O Abu al-Misk — this is the face I yearned for, and this is the day I hoped for.
context: Composed upon Al-Mutanabbi's arrival in Egypt, this poem was initially a panegyric for Kafur al-Ikhshidi, whom he praised in hopes of receiving a governorship. The famous opening — 'It is illness enough that you see death as a cure' — became one of the most quoted Arabic lines on despair and disillusionment. Ironically, Al-Mutanabbi later turned bitterly against Kafur when his hopes went unfulfilled, making these early praises a poignant contrast to his later devastating satires.

titleAr: أريك الرضى لو أخفت النفس خافيا
titleEn: I Show You Satisfaction, Though My Soul Hides Its Secrets

verses.sadr: أريك الرضى لو أخفت النفس خافيا
verses.ajuz: وما أنا عن نفسي ولا عنك راضيا
وجبنا ، أشخصا لحت لي أم مخازيا
وما أنا إلا ضاحك من رجائيا
رأيتك ذا نعل إذا كنت حافيا
من الجهل أم قد صار أبيض صافيا
بما كنت في سري به لك هاجيا

translationEn: I show you satisfaction — if only the soul could hide what is hidden — though I am pleased neither with myself nor with you. Faithfulness, then broken promises, treachery, baseness, and cowardice — are you a person I behold, or merely a collection of disgraces? You think my smiles are hope and delight, but I am only laughing at my own hopes. Your feet amuse me in their sandals — for I saw you as one who wears sandals only because you were once barefoot. You do not even know whether your skin is black from ignorance or has become purely white. Were it not for people's meddling, I would have come to you praising you with the very words I secretly used to mock you. So you were delighted with what I recite, even though in reciting it, your satire came at a high price. One like you is brought from distant lands to make the mourning women in their black garments laugh.
context: One of Al-Mutanabbi's most vicious satires against Kafur al-Ikhshidi, the ruler of Egypt. After years of unfulfilled promises, Al-Mutanabbi revealed that his earlier praises had been disguised mockery all along. The poem is shocking in its directness — attacking Kafur's former slave status, his race, and his intelligence. The final line, suggesting Kafur's very existence could make mourning women laugh, is among the cruelest closings in Arabic hija. This poem was composed as Al-Mutanabbi fled Egypt in 960 CE.
